| DTIME | Input | TIME | I, Q, M, D, L or constant | Delay time (1 to 60000 ms) <sup>1)</sup>  You can realize longer times, for example, by using a counter in a time-delay interrupt OB. |
| SIGN | Input | WORD | I, Q, M, D, L or constant | Identifier that appears when the time-delay interrupt OB is called in the start event information of the OB. |
| RET_VAL | Return | INT | I, Q, M, D, L | Status of the instruction |
| <sup>1)</sup> S7-1500 CPUs as of firmware version V1.7: 0 ms to 60000 ms |  |  |  |  |

You can find additional information on valid data types under "[Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types)".

##### Parameter RET_VAL

| Error code*   (W#16#...) | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 0000 | No error |
| 8090 | Incorrect parameter OB_NR |
| 8091 | Incorrect parameter DTIME |
| General error information | See also: [GET_ERR_ID: Get error ID locally](LAD%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#get_err_id-get-error-id-locally-s7-1200-s7-1500) |
| * The error codes in the program editor are displayed as integer or hexadecimal values. For information on switching the display formats, refer to "See also". |  |

##### Example

You can find the example here: [Program example for time-delay interrupt functions](#program-example-for-time-delay-interrupt-functions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

You can find additional information and the program code for the above named example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

---

**See also**

[Switching display formats in the program status](Testing%20the%20user%20program.md#switching-display-formats-in-the-program-status)

#### CAN_DINT: Cancel time-delay interrupt (S7-1200, S7-1500)

##### Description

You use this instruction to cancel a started time-delay interrupt and, thus, also cancel the call of the time-delay interrupt OB that is executed after the configured delay time. You specify the number of the organization block whose call is to be canceled in the OB_NR parameter.

##### Parameters

The following table shows the parameters of the "CAN_DINT" instruction:

| Parameter | Declaration | Data type | Memory area | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| OB_NR | Input | OB_DELAY (INT) | I, Q, M, D, L or constant | Number of the OB whose call will be canceled |
| RET_VAL | Return | INT | I, Q, M, D, L | Status of the instruction |

You can find additional information on valid data types under "[Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types)".

##### Parameter RET_VAL

| Error code*   (W#16#...) | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 0000 | No error |
| 8090 | Incorrect parameter OB_NR |
| 80A0 | Time-delay interrupt has not started. |
| * The error codes in the program editor are displayed as integer or hexadecimal values. For information on switching the display formats, refer to "See also". |  |

##### Example

You can find the example here: [Program example for time-delay interrupt functions](#program-example-for-time-delay-interrupt-functions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

You can find additional information and the program code for the above named example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

#### QRY_DINT: Query time-delay interrupt status (S7-1200, S7-1500)

##### Description

The instruction "QRY_DINT" is used to query the status of the time-delay interrupt.

##### Parameters

The following table shows the parameters of the "QRY_DINT" instruction:

| Parameter | Declaration | Data type | Memory area | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| OB_NR | Input | OB_DELAY (INT) | I, Q, M, D, L or constant | Number of the OB whose status is being queried. |
| RET_VAL | Return | INT | I, Q, M, D, L | If an error occurs while the instruction is being executed, the actual parameter of RET_VAL contains an error code. The value "0" is displayed in the STATUS parameter. |
| STATUS | Output | WORD | I, Q, M, D, L | Status of the time-delay interrupt, see  following table. |

You can find additional information on valid data types under "[Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types)".

##### Parameter STATUS

| Bit | Value | Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 0 | 0 | Irrelevant |
| 1 | 0 | Time-delay interrupt is enabled by the operating system. |
|  | 1 | Time-delay interrupt is disabled. |
| 2 | 0 | Time-delay interrupt is not activated or has elapsed. |
|  | 1 | Time-delay interrupt is activated. |
| 3 | - | - |
| 4 | 0 | Time-delay interrupt OB with the specified number does not exist. |
|  | 1 | Time-delay interrupt OB with the specified number exists. |
| Other bits |  | Always "0" |

##### Parameter RET_VAL

| Error code*  (W#16#...) | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 0000 | No error occurred. |
| 8090 | Incorrect information in the OB_NR parameter |
| General error information | See also: [GET_ERR_ID: Get error ID locally](LAD%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#get_err_id-get-error-id-locally-s7-1200-s7-1500) |
| * The error codes in the program editor are displayed as integer or hexadecimal values. For information on switching the display formats, refer to "See also". |  |

##### Example

You can find the example here: [Program example for time-delay interrupt functions](#program-example-for-time-delay-interrupt-functions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

You can find additional information and the program code for the above named example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

---

**See also**

[Switching display formats in the program status](Testing%20the%20user%20program.md#switching-display-formats-in-the-program-status)

#### Program example for time-delay interrupt functions (S7-1200, S7-1500)

##### Introduction

In the following example, you read the status of a time-delay interrupt OB (OB20), and set a new delay time and start or disable OB20.

##### Requirement

Create 10 tags and a structure in a global data block for storing the data.

![Requirement](images/95832774795_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Interconnect parameters: "SLI_itemiseStatus_DINT" FC

To evaluate the status information, create an FC "SLI_itemiseStatus_DINT".

Create the following local tag in it.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_itemiseStatus_DINT" FC](images/95832892683_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

Create interconnections for all relevant bits.

Network 1:

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_itemiseStatus_DINT" FC](images/95832881291_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

Network 2:

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_itemiseStatus_DINT" FC](images/95832885003_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

Network 3:

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_itemiseStatus_DINT" FC](images/95832888715_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FB_SRT_DINT" FB

To call the instructions, create an FB "SLI_FB_SRT_DINT". Create the following local tags in the function block "SLI_FB_SRT_DINT".

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FB_SRT_DINT" FB](images/95832778507_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

Network 1: Interconnect the parameters of the "QRY_DINT" instruction as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FB_SRT_DINT" FB](images/95832873867_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

Network 2: Interconnect the parameters of the "SRT_DINT" instruction as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FB_SRT_DINT" FB](images/82192064011_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

Network 3: Interconnect the parameters of the "CAN_DINT" instruction as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FB_SRT_DINT" FB](images/82192162443_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

Network 4: Interconnect the parameters that are to be reset with the execution of "CAN_DINT" as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FB_SRT_DINT" FB](images/82192171275_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

Network 5: Interconnect the parameter of the "SLI_itemiseStatus_DINT" FC as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FB_SRT_DINT" FB](images/95832877579_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

You call the FB "SLI_FB_SRT_DINT" in a cyclic OB (OB1).

##### Interconnect parameters: OB20

Create the following interconnections in OB20.

![Interconnect parameters: OB20](images/82192020747_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Result of "QRY_DINT"

The instructions receive the number of the OB to be called (OB20) with the input parameter OB_NR ("obNbr").

**Network 1:**

When the normally open contact ("executeQry") supplies the signal state "TRUE", the "QRY_DINT" instruction is executed and the data of OB20 is read. The output parameter STATUS ("#statWord") displays the status of OB20. The output parameter RET_VAL ("returnValueQry") indicates that processing is taking place without errors.

![Result of "QRY_DINT"](images/95832866443_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

The value of the tag "#statWord" is mapped in the structure "itemiseStatus".

![Result of "QRY_DINT"](images/95832862731_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Result of "SRT_DINT"

Network 2: When the normally open contact ("executeSrt") supplies the signal state "TRUE", the "SRT_DINT" instruction is executed. The input parameter DTIME ("delayTime") transfers the delay time (6 s) to OB20 and OB20 is enabled.

> **Note**
>
> Its delay time starts running at the time OB20 is enabled. OB20 is then called unless it has received the delay time again beforehand in a new CPU cycle.

The selected identifier (value "16#0001") to be output with the execution of OB20 is stored at the input parameter SIGN ("sign"). The identifier is shown in the start event information of OB20. The output parameter RET_VAL ("returnValueSrt") indicates that processing took place without errors.

If the NO contact ("executeSrt") returns the signal state "FALSE" after successful processing of SRT_DINT, the delay time is not transferred again to the OB20. As soon as the delay time has expired, OB20 is executed once.

> **Note**
>
> The described reaction of the OB20 in combination with the EN parameter only applies when the currently used CPU cycle time is less than the delay time of the OB20.

##### Result of the OB20

With each call of OB20, the instruction "ADD" is executed and the tag "testCurrCount" is incremented by "1". OB20 is called only once after expiration of the delay time defined with SRT_DINT. After it has been called, OB20 is once again "disabled".

![Result of the OB20](images/95832859019_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Result of "CAN_DINT"

Network 3: As long as OB20 is set and enabled ("#statWord" has the value "16#0014"), the comparator returns the signal state "TRUE" and CAN_DINT can be executed.

![Result of "CAN_DINT"](images/95832870155_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

When the normally open contact ("executeCan") supplies the signal state "TRUE", the "CAN_DINT" instruction is executed. The instruction disables OB20 and deletes the set call data of OB20.  
The output parameter RET_VAL ("returnValueCan") indicates that processing took place without errors.

The execution of CAN_DINT sets the coil ("#statMem") to "TRUE" and network 4 is executed.

##### Result of the reset

Network 4: When the normally open contact ("#statMem") supplies the signal state "TRUE", the tag "testCurrCount" is reset to the value "0" with the instruction "MOVE". Reset ("executeCan") in the signal state "TRUE" also automatically ends the execution of the instruction "CAN_DINT".

##### Result of the status information "#statWord"

The FC "SLI_itemiseStatus_DINT" is called in network 7. The significant bits of the tag "#statWord" are evaluated and output as Boolean tags with plain-text name. When a tag of the structure "itemiseStatus" supplies the signal state "TRUE", its statement is correct.

##### Program code

You can find additional information and the program code for the above-named example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

### Synchronous errors (S7-1500)

This section contains information on the following topics:

- [Mask synchronous error events (S7-1500)](#mask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)
- [MSK_FLT: Mask synchronous error events (S7-1500)](#msk_flt-mask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)
- [DMSK_FLT: Unmask synchronous error events (S7-1500)](#dmsk_flt-unmask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)
- [READ_ERR: Read out event status register (S7-1500)](#read_err-read-out-event-status-register-s7-1500)
- [Program example for synchronous error events (S7-1500)](#program-example-for-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)

#### Mask synchronous error events (S7-1500)

##### Introduction

Synchronous errors are programming and access errors. Such errors occur as a result of programming with incorrect operand areas or numbers, or incorrect addresses. **Masking** these synchronous errors means the following:

- Masked synchronous errors do not trigger an error OB call and do not lead to a programmed alternative reaction.
- The CPU "records" the masked errors that have occurred in an error status register.

Masking is carried out by calling the "[MSK_FLT](#msk_flt-mask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)" instruction.

**Unmasking** errors means canceling a previously set mask and clearing the corresponding bit in the event status register of the current priority class. Masking is canceled as follows:

- By calling the "[DMSK_FLT](#dmsk_flt-unmask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)" instruction.
- Once the current priority class has been completed.

If an error event occurs after it has been unmasked, the operating system will start the associated error OB.

You can use the "[READ_ERR](#read_err-read-out-event-status-register-s7-1500)" instruction to read the masked errors that have occurred.

> **Note**
>
> Regardless of masking or unmasking of the error events for the S7-1500, the error event is entered in the diagnostic buffer and the group error LED of the CPU lights up.

##### Handling errors in general

Different actions are possible if programming and I/O area access errors occur in a user program:

- You can program an error OB that is called by the operating system when the corresponding error occurs.
- You can disable the error OB call individually for each priority class. If an error of this type occurs in the priority class, the CPU does not change to STOP in this case. The CPU enters the error in an error register. From this entry, however, you do not recognize when or how often this error has occurred.

  ![Handling errors in general](images/63734311947_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-en-US.png)

**Synchronous errors** are assigned to a particular bit pattern known as the **error filter**. You can find this error filter in the input and output parameters of the "[MSK_FLT](#msk_flt-mask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)", "[DMSK_FLT](#dmsk_flt-unmask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)", and "[READ_ERR](#read_err-read-out-event-status-register-s7-1500)" instructions.

Synchronous errors are divided into **programming and access errors** which you can mask in two error masks. The error filters are illustrated in the following figures.

##### **Programming error filter**

The following figure shows the bit pattern of the error filter for programming errors. The error filter for the programming errors is available in the "PRGFLT_..." parameters (see below "Programming errors, Low-Word" or "Programming errors, High-Word").

![Programming error filter](images/76052787467_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-en-US.png)

The non-relevant bits "x" are set to "0" for the input and output parameters of the instructions "[MSK_FLT](#msk_flt-mask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)", "[DMSK_FLT](#dmsk_flt-unmask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)", "[READ_ERR](#read_err-read-out-event-status-register-s7-1500)".

##### **Access error mask**

The following figure shows the bit pattern of the error mask for access errors. The error mask for access errors is located in the parameters ACCFLT_...

![Access error mask](images/63734320907_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-en-US.png)

**Example:** The following figure shows how the low word of the error mask for access errors appears with all masked errors.

- As input parameter for "[MSK_FLT](#msk_flt-mask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)"
- As output parameter for "[MSK_FLT](#msk_flt-mask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)"

  ![Access error mask](images/63734325387_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-en-US.png)

##### Programming error low word

The following table lists the errors assigned to the low word of the error filter for programming errors. The possible causes of the errors are also listed.

| Error | Event ID (W#16#...) | Possible cause of error |
| --- | --- | --- |
| BCD conversion error | 2521 | The value to be converted is not a BCD number (e.g. 5E8). |
| Area length error when reading | 2522 | An addressed operand is not located entirely within the possible operand range.  Example: MW 320 should be read although the memory area  is only 256 bytes long. |
| Area length error when writing | 2523 | An addressed operand is not located entirely within the possible operand range.  Example: A value should be written to MW 320 although the  memory area is only 256 bytes long. |
| Area error when reading | 2524 | An incorrect range identifier for the operand was specified during indirect, overlapping range addressing.  Example:   - Correct: LAR1 P#E 12.0   L W[AR1, P#0.0] - Incorrect: LAR1 P#12.0   L W[AR1, P#0.0] The area error is reported during this operation. |
| Area error when writing | 2525 | An incorrect range identifier for the operand was specified during indirect, overlapping range addressing.  Example:   - Correct: LAR1 P#E 12.0   T W[AR1, P#0.0] - Incorrect: LAR1 P#12.0   T W[AR1, P#0.0] The area error is reported during this operation. |
| Timer number error (not implemented with the S7-1500) | 2526 | A non-existent timer is accessed.  Example:  - SI T [MW 0] with MW 0 = 129; the timer 129 should be started although there are only 128 timers available. |
| Counter number error (not implemented with the S7-1500) | 2527 | A non-existent counter is accessed.  - Example: ZV Z [MW 0] with MW 0 = 600; the counter 600 is accessed although only 512 counters are available. |
| Alignment error when reading | 2528 | A byte, word or double word operand with a bit address ≠ 0 is addressed.  Example:   - Correct: LAR1 P#M12.0   L B[AR1, P#0.0] - Incorrect: LAR1 P#M12.4   L B[AR1, P#0.0] |
| Alignment error when writing | 2529 | A byte, word or double word operand with a bit address ≠ 0 is addressed.  Example:   - Correct: LAR1 P#M12.0   T B[AR1, P#0.0] - Incorrect: LAR1 P#M12.4   T B[AR1, P#0.0] |

##### Programming error high word

The following table lists the errors assigned to the high word of the error filter for programming errors. The possible causes of the errors are also listed.

| Error | Event ID (W#16#...) | Possible cause of error |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Write error data block | 2530 | The data block to be written to is write-protected. |
| Write error instance data block | 2531 | The instance data block to be written to is write-protected. |
| Block number error DB | 2532 | The number of the data block is greater than the highest permissible number. |
| Block number error DI | 2533 | The number of the instance data block is greater than the highest permissible number. |
| Block number error FC | 2534 | The number of a called function (FC) is greater than the highest permissible number. |
| Block number error FB | 2535 | The number of a called function block (FB) is greater than the highest permissible number. |
| DB not loaded | 253A | The data block is not loaded. |
| Instruction not loaded | 253C to 253F | The instruction to be called is not loaded. |

##### Access error

The following table contains the errors that are assigned to the error mask for access errors. The possible causes of the errors are also listed.

| Error | Event ID (W#16#...) | Possible cause of error |
| --- | --- | --- |
| I/O access error  when reading | 2942 | - The address in the I/O area has no assigned signal module. - Access to this I/O area was not acknowledged within the specified module monitoring time (timeout). |
| I/O access error  when writing | 2943 |  |

#### MSK_FLT: Mask synchronous error events (S7-1500)

##### Description

You use the instruction to control the reaction of the CPU to synchronous errors. This is done by masking the respective synchronous errors (for error filters, see [Mask synchronous error events](#mask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)). When you call "MSK_FLT", you mask the synchronous errors in the current priority class.

If you set individual bits of the synchronous error filter to "1" in the input parameters, other bits that were set previously retain their value of "1". You obtain new error filters that you read out using the output parameters. The synchronous errors you have masked do not call an OB but are simply entered in an error register. You read the event status register with the instruction "[READ_ERR](#read_err-read-out-event-status-register-s7-1500)".

##### Parameters

The following table shows the parameters of the instruction "MSK_FLT":

| Parameter | Declaration | Data type | Memory area | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| PRGFLT_SET_MASK | Input | DWORD | I, Q, M, D, L or constant | Programming error to be  masked |
| ACCFLT_SET_MASK | Input | DWORD | I, Q, M, D, L or constant | Access error to be masked |
| RET_VAL | Return | INT | I, Q, M, D, L | Error information |
| PRGFLT_MASKED | Output | DWORD | I, Q, M, D, L | Masked programming errors |
| ACCFLT_MASKED | Output | DWORD | I, Q, M, D, L | Masked access errors |

You can find additional information on valid data types under "[Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types)".

##### Parameter RET_VAL

| Error code*  (W#16#...) | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 0000 | None of the errors were already masked. |
| 0001 | At least one of the errors was already masked. Nevertheless, the other errors will be masked. |
| - | General error information  See also: [Getting error ID locally with GetErrorID](SCL%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#get_err_id-get-error-id-locally-s7-1200-s7-1500) |
| * The error codes in the program editor are displayed as integer or hexadecimal values. For information on switching the display formats, refer to "See also". |  |

##### Example

You can find the example here: [Program example for synchronous error events](#program-example-for-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500).

You can find additional information and the program code for the example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

---

**See also**

[Evaluating errors with output parameter RET_VAL](Programming%20basics.md#evaluating-errors-with-output-parameter-ret_val)
  
[Switching display formats in the program status](Testing%20the%20user%20program.md#switching-display-formats-in-the-program-status)

#### DMSK_FLT: Unmask synchronous error events (S7-1500)

##### Description

You use the instruction to unmask the errors masked with "[MSK_FLT](#msk_flt-mask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)". To do this, you must set the corresponding bits of the error filter to "1" in the input parameters. With the "DMSK_FLT" call, you unmask the corresponding synchronous errors of the current priority class. At the same time, the queried entries are cleared in the error register. You read out the new error filters using the output parameters.

##### Parameters

The following table shows the parameters of the instruction "DMSK_FLT":

| Parameter | Declaration | Data type | Memory area | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| PRGFLT_RESET_MASK | Input | DWORD | I, Q, M, D, L or constant | Programming errors to be  unmasked |
| ACCFLT_RESET_MASK | Input | DWORD | I, Q, M, D, L or constant | Access errors to be  unmasked |
| RET_VAL | Return | INT | I, Q, M, D, L | Error information |
| PRGFLT_MASKED | Output | DWORD | I, Q, M, D, L | Still masked programming  errors |
| ACCFLT_MASKED | Output | DWORD | I, Q, M, D, L | Still masked access errors |

You can find additional information on valid data types under "[Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types)".

##### Parameter RET_VAL

| Error code* (W#16#...) | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 0000 | All specified errors were unmasked. |
| 0001 | At least one of the errors was not masked. Nevertheless, the other errors will be unmasked. |
| - | General error information  See also: [GET_ERR_ID: Get error ID locally](SCL%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#get_err_id-get-error-id-locally-s7-1200-s7-1500) |
| * The error codes in the program editor are displayed as integer or hexadecimal values. For information on switching the display formats, refer to "See also". |  |

##### Example

You can find the example here: [Program example for synchronous error events](#program-example-for-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500).

You can find additional information and the program code for the example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

---

**See also**

[Switching display formats in the program status](Testing%20the%20user%20program.md#switching-display-formats-in-the-program-status)

#### READ_ERR: Read out event status register (S7-1500)

##### Description

You use the instruction to read the error register. The structure of the error register corresponds to that of the programming and access error masks, which you can program as input parameters with "[MSK_FLT](#msk_flt-mask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)" and "[DMSK_FLT](#dmsk_flt-unmask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)".

In the input parameters, you enter the synchronous errors you want to read from the error register. When you call "READ_ERR", you read the required entries from the error register and at the same time clear these entries.

The error register contains information that tells you which of the masked synchronous errors in the current priority class occurred at least once. If a bit is set, this means that the corresponding masked synchronous error occurred at least once.

##### Parameters

The following table shows the parameters of the instruction "READ_ERR":

| Parameter | Declaration | Data type | Memory area | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| PRGFLT_QUERY | Input | DWORD | I, Q, M, D, L or constant | Query programming error |
| ACCFLT_QUERY | Input | DWORD | I, Q, M, D, L or constant | Query access error |
| RET_VAL | Return | INT | I, Q, M, D, L | Error information |
| PRGFLT_CLR | Output | DWORD | I, Q, M, D, L | Programming errors that occurred |
| ACCFLT_CLR | Output | DWORD | I, Q, M, D, L | Access errors that occurred |

You can find additional information on valid data types under "[Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types)".

##### Parameter RET_VAL

| Error code*  (W#16#...) | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 0000 | All queried errors are masked. |
| 0001 | At least one of the queried errors is not masked. |
| - | General error information  See also: [Getting error ID locally with GetErrorID](SCL%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#get_err_id-get-error-id-locally-s7-1200-s7-1500) |
| * The error codes in the program editor are displayed as integer or hexadecimal values. For information on switching the display formats, refer to "See also". |  |

##### Example

You can find the example here: [Program example for synchronous error events](#program-example-for-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500).

You can find additional information and the program code for the example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

---

**See also**

[Evaluating errors with output parameter RET_VAL](Programming%20basics.md#evaluating-errors-with-output-parameter-ret_val)
  
[Switching display formats in the program status](Testing%20the%20user%20program.md#switching-display-formats-in-the-program-status)

#### Program example for synchronous error events (S7-1500)

##### Introduction

In the example below, mask the synchronous error events (program errors) with the instruction "MSK_FLT", unmask a part of the synchronous error events with the instruction "DMSK_FLT" and read out the event status register with the instruction "READ_ERR". When the program error is unmasked, call a program error OB.

##### Storage of data

Create the following structures and tags for storing the data in a global data block.

- Tags for MSK_FLT:

  ![Storage of data](images/95758495883_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)
- MSK_FLT / content of the structure "progErrMask_Details":

  ![Storage of data](images/95758718347_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

  You can find the complete organization of the structure here: [Mask synchronous error events](#mask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500).
- MSK_FLT / content of the structure "accessErrMask_Details":

  ![Storage of data](images/95758658315_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)
- Tags for DMSK_FLT:

  ![Storage of data](images/95758727179_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)
- Tags for READ_ERR:

  ![Storage of data](images/95758748811_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

  The organization of the structures "occProgErrMask_Details" and "occAccErrMask_Details" is similar to the organization of the structures "progErrMask_Details" and "accessErrMask_Details".
- Tags for the test result:

  ![Storage of data](images/95758783243_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FB_genErr_SyncErrEv" FB

Create an "SLI_FB_genErr_SyncErrEv" FB to generate a program error.

Create the following local tag in it.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FB_genErr_SyncErrEv" FB](images/95759153163_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

Create the following interconnections in the FB.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FB_genErr_SyncErrEv" FB](images/95759161995_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Interconnect parameters: "SLI_itemiseDW_SyncErrEv" FC

To transfer a bit sequence in the size of a DWORD, create the FC "SLI_itemiseDW_SyncErrEv".

Create the following local tags (inputs and outputs from 1 to 31) in it.

- Excerpt of the inputs:

  ![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_itemiseDW_SyncErrEv" FC](images/95759170827_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)
- Excerpt of the outputs:

  ![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_itemiseDW_SyncErrEv" FC](images/95759179659_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

  ![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_itemiseDW_SyncErrEv" FC](images/95759188491_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

Create the following interconnections for the inputs and outputs in the FC based on the example of network 1.

**Network 1:**

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_itemiseDW_SyncErrEv" FC](images/95759222923_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Interconnect parameters: "SLI_mask_SyncErrEv" FC

To convert the masks to be set for program and access errors, create the FC "SLI_mask_SyncErrEv".

Create the following local tags in it.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_mask_SyncErrEv" FC](images/95759244555_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 1:**

Interconnect the parameters of the "SLI_itemiseDW_SyncErrEv" FC according to the following example.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_mask_SyncErrEv" FC](images/95759253387_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

You can find the complete organization of the structure of the program errors ("progErrMask_Details") here: [Mask synchronous error events](#mask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500).

**Network 2:**

Interconnect bit 2 of the DWORD of the access errors as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_mask_SyncErrEv" FC](images/95759300619_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 3:**

Interconnect bit 3 of the DWORD of the access errors as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_mask_SyncErrEv" FC](images/95759309451_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Interconnect parameters: "SLI_occured_SyncErrEv" FC

To convert read program and access errors, create the FC "SLI_occured_SyncErrEv".

Create the following local tags in it.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_occured_SyncErrEv" FC](images/95759422475_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 1:**

Interconnect the parameters of the "SLI_itemiseDW_SyncErrEv" FC according to the following example.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_occured_SyncErrEv" FC](images/95759431307_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

You can find the complete organization of the structure of the program errors ("occProgErrMask_Details") here: [Mask synchronous error events](#mask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500).

**Network 2:**

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_occured_SyncErrEv" FC](images/95759440139_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 3:**

Interconnect bit 3 of the DWORD of the access errors as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_occured_SyncErrEv" FC](images/95759448971_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Interconnect parameters: "SLI_peOB_SyncErrEV" OB

Create the OB "SLI_peOB_SyncErrEV" in case of a program error in the unmasked state.

**Network 1:**

Create the following interconnection in the OB.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_peOB_SyncErrEV" OB](images/95759457803_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT" FC

To call the program parts, create the FC "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT".

**Network 1:**

Interconnect the parameters of the FC "SLI_mask_SyncErrEv" as well as the tags "peOB_unmased_active", "MSK_FLT.execute" and "maskedProgErrs" as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT" FC](images/95759318283_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 2:**

Interconnect the parameters of the "MSK_FLT" instruction as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT" FC](images/95759339915_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 3:**

Interconnect the parameters of the "DMSK_FLT" instruction as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT" FC](images/95759361547_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 4:**

Interconnect the parameters of the "SLI_FB_genErr_SyncErrEv" FC as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT" FC](images/95759370379_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 5:**

Interconnect the parameters of the "READ_ERR" instruction as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT" FC](images/95759404811_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 6:**

Interconnect the parameters of the "SLI_occured_SyncErrEv" FC as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT" FC](images/95759413643_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Result of OB "SLI_peOB_SyncErrEv"

When a program error was triggered and is not masked by a filter, the program error OB "SLI_peOB_SyncErrEv" is executed.

The tag "READ_ERR.peOB_unmasked_active" is then set to "TRUE" in network 1 of the OB.

##### Result of FC "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT"

**Network 1 (FC "SLI_mask_SyncErrEv"):**

By calling the FC "SLI_mask_SyncErrEv", the structures for program errors and access errors are converted into the data type DWORD ("progErrSetMask" and "accessErrSetMask").

Reset the value because the value "TRUE" of the tag "READ_ERR.peOB_unmasked_active" is still displayed even after calling the program error OB. If the instruction "MSK_FLT" is not executed successfully ("MSK_FLT.execute" has the value "FALSE"), the tag "maskedProgErrs" is reset.

**Network 2 ("MSK_FLT"):**

When the normally open contact ("MSK_FLT.execute") supplies the signal state "TRUE", the "MSK_FLT" instruction is executed.

The synchronous error events that have to be controlled are specified according to the tags "progErrMask_Details" and "accessErrMask_Details":

- You specify the program errors you want to mask with "progErrMask_Details".
- No access errors ("accessErrMask_Details") were masked in the example.

The synchronous error events that have to be controlled are stored in the data type DWORD with the input parameters PRGFLT_SET_MASK ("MSK_FLT.progErrSetMask") and ACCFLT_SET_MASK ("MSK_FLT.accessErrSetMask").

The output parameter RET_VAL ("MSK_FLT.returnValue") indicates that none of the errors was already masked. The currently masked synchronous error events are displayed at the output parameters "PRGFLT_MASKED" ("maskedProgErrs") and ACCFLT_MASKED ("maskedAccessErrs").

![Result of FC "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT"](images/95758856971_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 3 ("DMSK_FLT"):**

When the normally open contact ("DMSK_FLT.execute") supplies the signal state "TRUE", the "DMSK_FLT" instruction is executed. The synchronous error events that have to be unmasked are stored in the data type DWORD with the input parameters PRGFLT_RESET_MASK ("DMSK_FLT.progErrResetMask") and ACCFLT_RESET_MASK ("DMSK_FLT.accessErrResetMask").

Output parameter RET_VAL ("DMSK_FLT.returnValue") indicates that all specified errors were unmasked.

![Result of FC "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT"](images/95758839307_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

The currently masked synchronous error events are displayed at the output parameters "PRGFLT_MASKED" ("maskedProgErrs") and ACCFLT_MASKED ("maskedAccessErrs").

![Result of FC "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT"](images/95758848139_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 4 (FB "SLI_FB_genErr_SyncErrEv"):**

When the tag "testErrorEvent.index" returns a value greater than "10", a program error is triggered.

**Network 5 ("READ_ERR"):**

The"READ_ERR" instruction reads the event status register. The synchronous error events that are to be read are stored in the data type DWORD with the input parameters PRGFLT_QUERY ("READ_ERR.progErrSetMask") and ACCFLT_QUERY ("READ_ERR.accessErrSetMask").

- Case 1 - The program error was not triggered. No masks are set:

  The output parameter RET_VAL ("READ_ERR.returnValue") indicates that at least one of the queried errors is not masked.

  The currently occurring error events are displayed at the output parameters "PRGFLT_CLR" ("READ_ERR.occuredMskProgErrs") and ACCFLT_CLR ("READ_ERR.occuredMskAccessErrs").

  ![Result of FC "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT"](images/95758904203_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)
- Case 2 - The program error was triggered and is not masked:

  MSK_FLT, DMSK_FLT are inactive. The program error OB is executed.

  ![Result of FC "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT"](images/95758913035_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)
- Case 3 - The triggered program error is masked:

  MSK_FLT is active. DMSK_FLT and the program error OB are inactive.

  ![Result of FC "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT"](images/95758981899_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

  The following program error occurred according to the structure "occProgErrMask_Details":

  ![Result of FC "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT"](images/95758921867_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)
- Case 4 - The triggered program error is unmasked:

  MSK_FLT, DMSK_FLT and the program error OB are active.

  ![Result of FC "SLI_FC_MSK_FLT"](images/95759144331_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 6 (FC "SLI_occured_SyncErrEv"):**

By calling the FC "SLI_occured_SyncErrEv", the read program errors ("occuredMskProgErrs") and access errors ("occuredMskAccessErrs") are converted into the structures "occProgErrMask_Details" and "occAccErrMask_Details".

##### Program code

You can find additional information and the program code for the above-named example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

---

**See also**

[MSK_FLT: Mask synchronous error events (S7-1500)](#msk_flt-mask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)
  
[DMSK_FLT: Unmask synchronous error events (S7-1500)](#dmsk_flt-unmask-synchronous-error-events-s7-1500)
  
[READ_ERR: Read out event status register (S7-1500)](#read_err-read-out-event-status-register-s7-1500)

### Asynchronous error event (S7-1200, S7-1500)

This section contains information on the following topics:

- [DIS_IRT: Disable interrupt event (S7-1500)](#dis_irt-disable-interrupt-event-s7-1500)
- [EN_IRT: Enable interrupt event (S7-1500)](#en_irt-enable-interrupt-event-s7-1500)
- [Program example for DIS_IRT & EN_IRT (S7-1200, S7-1500)](#program-example-for-dis_irt-en_irt-s7-1200-s7-1500)
- [DIS_AIRT: Delay execution of higher priority interrupts and asynchronous error events (S7-1200, S7-1500)](#dis_airt-delay-execution-of-higher-priority-interrupts-and-asynchronous-error-events-s7-1200-s7-1500)
- [EN_AIRT: Enable execution of higher priority interrupts and asynchronous error events (S7-1200, S7-1500)](#en_airt-enable-execution-of-higher-priority-interrupts-and-asynchronous-error-events-s7-1200-s7-1500)
- [Program example for DIS_AIRT & EN_AIRT (S7-1200, S7-1500)](#program-example-for-dis_airt-en_airt-s7-1200-s7-1500)

#### DIS_IRT: Disable interrupt event (S7-1500)

##### Description

The instruction "DIS_IRT" is used to disable the processing of new interrupts and asynchronous error events. Disabling means that if an interrupting event occurs, the operating system of the CPU reacts as follows:

- It **neither** calls an interrupt OB nor an asynchronous error OB,
- **nor** does it trigger the normal reaction if an interrupt OB or asynchronous error OB is not programmed.

If you disable interrupts and asynchronous error events, this remains in effect for all priority classes. The disable is only canceled with the instruction "[EN_IRT](#en_irt-enable-interrupt-event-s7-1500)" or by a warm or a cold restart.

> **Note**
>
> Remember that when you program the "DIS_IRT" instruction, all interrupts that occur will be discarded.

##### Parameters

The following table shows the parameters of the "DIS_IRT" instruction:

| Parameter | Declaration | Data type | Memory area | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| MODE | Input | BYTE | I, Q, M, D, L or constant | Specifies which interrupts and asynchronous  errors are disabled. |
| OB_NR | Input | INT | I, Q, M, D, L or constant | OB number |
| RET_VAL | Return | INT | I, Q, M, D, L | If an error occurs while the instruction is being executed, the return value contains an error code. |

You can find additional information on valid data types under "[Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types)".

##### Parameter MODE

| MODE (B#16#...) | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| 00 | All newly occurring interrupts and asynchronous error events are disabled (with the exception of the programming error and the error during direct access to I/O data and the Motion Control OBs). Assign the value "0" to the OB_NR parameter. Entries continue to be made in the diagnostics buffer. |
| 01 | All newly occurring events belonging to a specified interrupt class are disabled. Identify the interrupt class by specifying it as follows:  - Time-of-day interrupts: 10 - Time-delay interrupts: 20 - Cyclic interrupts: 30 - Process interrupts: 40 - Interrupts for DPV1: 50 - Synchronous cycle interrupt: 60 - Redundancy error interrupts: 70 - Asynchronous error interrupts: 80   Entries continue to be made in the diagnostics buffer. |
| 02 | All new occurrences of a specified interrupt are disabled. You designate the interrupt  using the OB number. Entries continue to be made in the diagnostics buffer. |

##### Parameter RET_VAL

| Error code*  (W#16#...) | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 0000 | No error occurred. |
| 8090 | The OB_NR input parameter contains an invalid value. |
| 8091 | The MODE input parameter contains an invalid value. |
| General error information | See also: [GET_ERR_ID: Get error ID locally](LAD%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#get_err_id-get-error-id-locally-s7-1200-s7-1500) |
| * The error codes in the program editor are displayed as integer or hexadecimal values. For information on switching the display formats, refer to "See also". |  |

##### Example

You can find the example here: [Program example for DIS_IRT & EN_IRT](#program-example-for-dis_irt-en_irt-s7-1200-s7-1500).

You can find additional information and the program code for the example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

#### EN_IRT: Enable interrupt event (S7-1500)

##### Description

You use the instruction to enable the processing of new interrupts and asynchronous error events that you have previously disabled with the "[DIS_IRT](#dis_irt-disable-interrupt-event-s7-1500)" instruction. This means that if an interrupt event occurs, the CPU operating system reacts in one of the following ways:

- It calls an interrupt OB or asynchronous error OB.

  or
- it triggers the specified reaction if an interrupt OB or asynchronous error OB is not programmed.

##### Parameters

The following table shows the parameters of the "EN_IRT" instruction:

| Parameter | Declaration | Data type | Memory area | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| MODE | Input | BYTE | I, Q, M, D, L or constant | Specifies which interrupts and  asynchronous error events will be enabled. |
| OB_NR | Input | INT | I, Q, M, D, L or constant | OB number |
| RET_VAL | Return | INT | I, Q, M, D, L | If an error occurs while the instruction is being executed, the return value contains an error code. |

You can find additional information on valid data types under "[Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types)".

##### Parameter MODE

| MODE | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| 0 | All newly occurring interrupts and asynchronous error events are enabled with the exception of the programming error and the error during direct access to I/O data and the Motion Control OBs. |
| 1 | All newly occurring events belonging to a specified interrupt class are enabled. Identify the interrupt class by specifying it as follows:  - Time-of-day interrupts: 10 - Time-delay interrupts: 20 - Cyclic interrupts: 30 - Process interrupts: 40 - Interrupts for DPV1: 50 - Synchronous cycle interrupt: 60 - Redundancy error interrupts: 70 - Asynchronous error interrupts: 80 |
| 2 | All newly occurring events of a specified interrupt are enabled. You designate the interrupt  using the OB number. |

##### Parameter RET_VAL

| Error code*  (W#16#...) | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 0000 | No error occurred. |
| 8090 | The OB_NR input parameter contains an invalid value. |
| 8091 | The MODE input parameter contains an invalid value. |
| General error information | See also: [GET_ERR_ID: Get error ID locally](LAD%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#get_err_id-get-error-id-locally-s7-1200-s7-1500) |
| * The error codes in the program editor are displayed as integer or hexadecimal values. For information on switching the display formats, refer to "See also". |  |

##### Example

You can find the example here: [Program example for DIS_IRT & EN_IRT](#program-example-for-dis_irt-en_irt-s7-1200-s7-1500).

You can find additional information and the program code for the example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

#### Program example for DIS_IRT & EN_IRT (S7-1200, S7-1500)

##### Introduction

In the following example you disable processing of a cyclic interrupt OB (OB33) with the help of the "DIS_IRT" instruction and enable OB33 once again with the instruction "EN_IRT".

##### Storage of data

Create the following structures and tags for storing the data in a global data block.

![Storage of data](images/96070367371_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### "SLI_ciOB_AsyncEvent" OB

You create an "SLI_ciOB_AsyncEvent" OB and monitor its behavior during disabling/enabling.

**Network 1**: You now call the instruction "TP" in the OB and create the following interconnections.

!["SLI_ciOB_AsyncEvent" OB](images/96072533259_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 2**: Reset the tag "testOB.execute" as follows:

!["SLI_ciOB_AsyncEvent" OB](images/96072644491_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### "SLI_FC_AsyncEvent" FC

To call the program parts, create the FC "SLI_FC_AsyncEvent".

Create the following local tags in the FC.

!["SLI_FC_AsyncEvent" FC](images/96072297099_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 1**: Interconnect the parameters of the "DIS_IRT" instruction as follows.

!["SLI_FC_AsyncEvent" FC](images/96072382731_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 2**: Reset the tag "DIS_IRT.execute" as follows:

!["SLI_FC_AsyncEvent" FC](images/96072391563_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 3**: Interconnect the parameters of the "EN_IRT" instruction as follows.

!["SLI_FC_AsyncEvent" FC](images/96072451595_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 4**: Reset the tag "EN_IRT.execute" as follows:

!["SLI_FC_AsyncEvent" FC](images/96072524427_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Result of OB "SLI_ciOB_AsyncEvent"

If the normally open contact ("testOB.execute") supplies the signal state "TRUE" once, the "TP" instruction is executed. The tag "testOB.execute" is automatically reset to the signal state "FALSE". The value for the "testTime" tag is incremented until it corresponds to the value of the "testTimeLimit" tag.

![Result of OB "SLI_ciOB_AsyncEvent"](images/96071246603_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Result of FC "SLI_FC_AsyncEvent"

**Network 1:**

If the normally open contact ("DIS_IRT.execute") supplies the signal state "TRUE", the "DIS_IRT" instruction is executed. The operating mode "2" to be used is specified according to the input parameter MODE ("DIS_IRT.mode"). OB33 is addressed and its processing disabled according to the input parameter OB_NR ("DIS_IRT.obNbr").

The output parameter RET_VAL ("DIS_IRT.returnValue") indicates that processing in the example is taking place without errors. You save the processing of "DIS_IRT" in the tag "DIS_IRT.done". You also reset the "EN_IRT.done" tag.

The value of the "testOB.testTime" tag is no longer incremented. Nevertheless, the IEC timer of the "TP" instruction continues to count in the background.

![Result of FC "SLI_FC_AsyncEvent"](images/96071255435_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 2:**

If the normally open contact ("#tempDIS") supplies the signal state "TRUE", the "DIS_IRT.execute" tag is reset.

**Network 3:**

If the normally open contact ("EN_IRT.execute") supplies the signal state "TRUE", the "EN_IRT" instruction is executed. The operating mode "2" to be used is specified according to the input parameter MODE ("EN_IRT.mode"). OB33 is addressed and its processing enabled once again according to the input parameter OB_NR ("EN_IRT.obNbr").

The output parameter RET_VAL ("EN_IRT.returnValue") indicates that processing in the example is taking place without errors. You save the processing of "EN_IRT" in the tag "EN_IRT.done". You also reset the "DIS_IRT.done" tag.

The value of the "testOB.testTime" tag is still incremented or changed according to the IEC timer of the "TP" instruction.

![Result of FC "SLI_FC_AsyncEvent"](images/96072275467_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 4:**

If the normally open contact ("#tempEN") supplies the signal state "TRUE", the "EN_IRT.execute" tag is reset.

##### Program code

You can find additional information and the program code for the above-named example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

---

**See also**

[DIS_IRT: Disable interrupt event (S7-1500)](#dis_irt-disable-interrupt-event-s7-1500)
  
[EN_IRT: Enable interrupt event (S7-1500)](#en_irt-enable-interrupt-event-s7-1500)

#### DIS_AIRT: Delay execution of higher priority interrupts and asynchronous error events (S7-1200, S7-1500)

##### Description

You use "DIS_AIRT" to delay the processing of interrupt OBs whose priority are higher than the priority of the current organization block.

You can call "DIS_AIRT" multiple times in an organization block. The "DIS_AIRT" calls are counted by the operating system. Processing is delayed more and more each time "DIS_AIRT" is executed. To cancel a delay, you must execute the "[EN_AIRT](#en_airt-enable-execution-of-higher-priority-interrupts-and-asynchronous-error-events-s7-1200-s7-1500)" instruction. The processing delay applies until the number of executions of "[EN_AIRT](#en_airt-enable-execution-of-higher-priority-interrupts-and-asynchronous-error-events-s7-1200-s7-1500)" matches the number of calls of "DIS_AIRT" or the current OB has been worked through.

Each call of "DIS_AIRT" increases the number of delays; the current value is made available to you at the RET_VAL parameter.

##### Parameters

The following table shows the parameters of the "DIS_AIRT" instruction:

| Parameter | Declaration | Data type | Memory area | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| RET_VAL | Return | INT | I, Q, M, D, L | Number of delays |

You can find additional information on valid data types under "[Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types)".

##### Parameter RET_VAL

| Return value | Description |
| --- | --- |
| n | "n" shows the number of processing delays, i.e., the number of DIS_AIRT calls, after the instruction is completed. Use [EN_AIRT](#en_airt-enable-execution-of-higher-priority-interrupts-and-asynchronous-error-events-s7-1200-s7-1500) to clear the delays. |

##### Example

You can find the example here: [Program example for DIS_AIRT & EN_AIRT](#program-example-for-dis_airt-en_airt-s7-1200-s7-1500).

You can find additional information and the program code for the example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

#### EN_AIRT: Enable execution of higher priority interrupts and asynchronous error events (S7-1200, S7-1500)

##### Description

You use "EN_AIRT" to enable processing of organization blocks when interrupts occur that have been delayed by the "[DIS_AIRT](#dis_airt-delay-execution-of-higher-priority-interrupts-and-asynchronous-error-events-s7-1200-s7-1500)" instruction.

When "EN_AIRT" is executed, you cancel a processing delay that was registered by the operating system when "[DIS_AIRT](#dis_airt-delay-execution-of-higher-priority-interrupts-and-asynchronous-error-events-s7-1200-s7-1500)" was called. To cancel all delays, the number of "EN_AIRT" executions must be equal to the number of "[DIS_AIRT](#dis_airt-delay-execution-of-higher-priority-interrupts-and-asynchronous-error-events-s7-1200-s7-1500)" calls. Example: You have called "[DIS_AIRT](#dis_airt-delay-execution-of-higher-priority-interrupts-and-asynchronous-error-events-s7-1200-s7-1500)" five times. As a result, the processing is also delayed five times. After this, you must call the "EN_AIRT" instruction five times in order to cancel all five delays.

In the EN_AIRT parameter of the "EN_AIRT" instruction, you query the number of interrupt delays that have not yet been enabled after the execution of "RET_VAL". The value "0" in the RET_VAL parameter means that all delays enabled by "[DIS_AIRT](#dis_airt-delay-execution-of-higher-priority-interrupts-and-asynchronous-error-events-s7-1200-s7-1500)" have been cancelled.

##### Parameters

The following table shows the parameters of the "EN_AIRT" instruction:

| Parameter | Declaration | Data type | Memory area | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| RET_VAL | Return | INT | I, Q, M, D, L | Number of configured delays |

You can find additional information on valid data types under "[Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types)".

##### Parameter RET_VAL

| Return value/  Error code*  (W#16#...) | Description |
| --- | --- |
| n | "n" shows the number of processing delays that are not yet enabled after the instruction is completed. If n = 0, interrupt handling is first enabled again. |
| 8080 | Although the interrupt processing was  already enabled, the function was called again. |
| General error information | See also: [GET_ERR_ID: Get error ID locally](LAD%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#get_err_id-get-error-id-locally-s7-1200-s7-1500) |
| * The error codes in the program editor are displayed as integer or hexadecimal values. For information on switching the display formats, refer to "See also". |  |

##### Example

You can find the example here: [Program example for DIS_AIRT & EN_AIRT](#program-example-for-dis_airt-en_airt-s7-1200-s7-1500).

You can find additional information and the program code for the example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

#### Program example for DIS_AIRT & EN_AIRT (S7-1200, S7-1500)

##### Introduction

In the following example you delay processing of a cyclic interrupt OB or of interrupt OBs with a higher priority with the help of the instructions "DIS_AIRT" and "EN_AIRT". A test program is not interrupted by an interrupt when you call the instructions.

You use the test program to save the time and diagnostics data in a data memory.

- To do this, you read out the CPU time with the instruction "TIME_TCK" and the diagnostics data with the instruction "GET_DIAG" in a cyclic interrupt OB.
- In a cyclic OB (OB1), you also read out the system time of the CPU clock with the instruction "RD_SYS_T".

##### Storage of data

Create the PLC data type "testAIRT" for the data memory.

![Storage of data](images/96429034635_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

Create the following structures and tags for storing the data in a global data block.

- For DIS_AIRT, EN_AIRT, and time recording:

  ![Storage of data](images/96427537675_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)
- For diagnostics:

  > **Note**
  >
  > **Find hardware identifier ("laddr")**
  >
  > Use "PLC tags > System constants" to find the hardware identifier. For an S7-1500 CPU: Find the entry "Local~Common" with the data type "HW_Submodule". The associated cell "value" contains the hardware identifier.

  ![Storage of data](images/96427546507_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)
- For the data memory:

  ![Storage of data](images/96427670539_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Interconnect parameters: in the FC "SLI_FC_itemise_IOState_AIRT"

To further evaluate the diagnostics data of the "GET_DIAG" instruction, create an FC "SLI_FC_itemise_IOState_AIRT".

Create the following local tags in the FC.

![Interconnect parameters: in the FC "SLI_FC_itemise_IOState_AIRT"](images/96428618251_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 1**: You create the following interconnections.

![Interconnect parameters: in the FC "SLI_FC_itemise_IOState_AIRT"](images/96428703883_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

![Interconnect parameters: in the FC "SLI_FC_itemise_IOState_AIRT"](images/96428712715_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Interconnect parameters: in the "SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT" OB

You create a cyclic interrupt OB "SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT" and monitor its behavior during the delay.

You create the following local tag in the OB.

![Interconnect parameters: in the "SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT" OB](images/96428185227_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 1**: You now call the instruction "GET_DIAG" in the OB and create the following interconnections.

![Interconnect parameters: in the "SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT" OB](images/96428194059_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 2**: You record the CPU time as follows and increment the "pos" tag according to the following condition.

![Interconnect parameters: in the "SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT" OB](images/96428305291_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 3**: Reset the tag "startOB_ci" as follows:

![Interconnect parameters: in the "SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT" OB](images/96428314123_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Interconnect parameters: in the FC "SLI_FC_saveDiag_AIRT"

To read the system time and merge the time and diagnostics data, create the FC "SLI_FC_saveDiag_AIRT".

Create the following local tags in the FC.

![Interconnect parameters: in the FC "SLI_FC_saveDiag_AIRT"](images/96428374155_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 1**: You create the following interconnections.

![Interconnect parameters: in the FC "SLI_FC_saveDiag_AIRT"](images/96428510987_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 2**: Reset the tag "startOB1" as follows:

![Interconnect parameters: in the FC "SLI_FC_saveDiag_AIRT"](images/96428519819_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Interconnect parameters: in the FC "SLI_FC_delayAIRT"

To call the test program, create the FC "SLI_FC_delayAIRT".

**Network 1**: Interconnect the parameters of the "DIS_AIRT" instruction as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: in the FC "SLI_FC_delayAIRT"](images/96427941131_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 2**: Interconnect the FC "SLI_FC_saveDiag_AIRT" as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: in the FC "SLI_FC_delayAIRT"](images/96428090763_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 3**: Interconnect the parameters of the "EN_AIRT" instruction as follows.

![Interconnect parameters: in the FC "SLI_FC_delayAIRT"](images/96428176395_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Procedure for starting the test scenario

To start the test program, follow these steps:

> **Note**
>
> If you do not want to delay processing of the cyclic interrupt OB ("SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT"), skip steps 1 and 2.

1. Set the tag "delay.executeDel" to "TRUE".
2. Set the tag "delay.executeEn" to "TRUE".
3. Set the tag "startOB1" to "TRUE". Afterwards, the tag "startOB_ci" is set automatically.

   The "startOB1" tag and the "startOB_ci" tag are automatically reset after filling the data memory.

##### Procedure for stopping the processing of DIS_AIRT and EN_AIRT

To stop the delayed processing of the cyclic interrupt OB ("SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT"), follow these steps:

1. Set the tag "delay.executeEn" to "FALSE".
2. Set the tag "delay.executeDel" to "FALSE".

##### Result of OB "SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT"

The cyclic interrupt OB ("SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT") is called every 100 ms; it reads the diagnostics data with the "GET_DIAG" instruction and outputs the CPU time.

If the normally open contact ("startOB_ci") supplies the signal state "TRUE" and the "pos" tag returns a value greater than "3", the "pos" tag is incremented. If the "pos" tag returns the value "3", the "startOB_ci" tag is reset.

![Result of OB "SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT"](images/96427743371_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

If execution of the cyclic interrupt OB ("SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT") is not delayed, the values of the data memory appear as below.

![Result of OB "SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT"](images/96427837835_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Result of FC "SLI_FC_delayAIRT"

**Network 1:**

If the normally open contact ("delay.executeDel") supplies the signal state "TRUE", the "DIS_AIRT" instruction is executed. Processing of the cyclic interrupt OB ("SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT") or of an interrupt OB with higher priority is delayed. The output parameter RET_VAL ("delay.nbrOfDelays") indicates that a delay is active in the calling OB (OB1).

**Network 2:**

If the normally open contact ("startOB1") supplies the signal state "TRUE", the test program of the FC "SLI_FC_saveDiag_AIRT" is executed.

**In the FC "SLI_FC_saveDiag_AIRT":**

The system time is read with the instruction "RD_SYS_T" and stored in the data memory "dataStorage". The cell of the data memory that is to be used is determined by the "pos" tag.

The CPU time ("outCPUtimer") is also stored in the data memory. If the "diag.itemiseIOState.Error" tag has the signal state "TRUE", the "errorHW" tag is set to "TRUE" in the data memory.

The following is decided in the next steps:

- If "pos" has the value "0": The value of the "startOB_ci" tag is set to "TRUE". This means the "pos" tag can be incremented in the cyclic interrupt OB ("SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT").
- If "pos" has the value "3": The tags "startOB1" and "pos" are reset.

If execution of the cyclic interrupt OB ("SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT") is delayed, the values of the data memory appear as below.

![Result of FC "SLI_FC_delayAIRT"](images/96427893899_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 3:**

If the normally open contact ("delay.executeEn") supplies the signal state "TRUE", the "EN_AIRT" instruction is executed. Processing of the cyclic interrupt OB ("SLI_ciOB_diagProg_AIRT") or of an interrupt OB with higher priority is enabled once again. The output parameter RET_VAL ("delay.nbrOfDelays") indicates that a delay is not active in the calling OB (OB1). Processing in the example takes place without errors.

![Result of FC "SLI_FC_delayAIRT"](images/96427885067_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

##### Program code

You can find additional information and the program code for the above-named example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

---

**See also**

[DIS_AIRT: Delay execution of higher priority interrupts and asynchronous error events (S7-1200, S7-1500)](#dis_airt-delay-execution-of-higher-priority-interrupts-and-asynchronous-error-events-s7-1200-s7-1500)
  
[EN_AIRT: Enable execution of higher priority interrupts and asynchronous error events (S7-1200, S7-1500)](#en_airt-enable-execution-of-higher-priority-interrupts-and-asynchronous-error-events-s7-1200-s7-1500)

## Alarms (S7-1200, S7-1500)

This section contains information on the following topics:

- [Program_Alarm: Generate program alarm with associated values (S7-1500)](#program_alarm-generate-program-alarm-with-associated-values-s7-1500)
- [Get_AlarmState: Output alarm status (S7-1500)](#get_alarmstate-output-alarm-status-s7-1500)
- [Gen_UsrMsg: Generate user diagnostic alarms (S7-1200, S7-1500)](#gen_usrmsg-generate-user-diagnostic-alarms-s7-1200-s7-1500)
- [Get_Alarm: Read pending alarm (S7-1500)](#get_alarm-read-pending-alarm-s7-1500)
- [Ack_Alarms: Acknowledge alarms (S7-1500)](#ack_alarms-acknowledge-alarms-s7-1500)
- [Program example for Get_Alarm & Ack_Alarms (S7-1200, S7-1500)](#program-example-for-get_alarm-ack_alarms-s7-1200-s7-1500)
- [Get_AlarmResources: Determine the number of available alarm instances (S7-1500)](#get_alarmresources-determine-the-number-of-available-alarm-instances-s7-1500)

### Program_Alarm: Generate program alarm with associated values (S7-1500)

#### Description

The instruction "Generate program alarm with associated values" monitors a signal and on a signal change at the parameterSIG generates a program alarm (for definition, see also: [Introduction to alarm configuration](Configuring%20alarms.md#introduction-to-alarm-configuration-s7-300-s7-400-s7-1500)). An incoming program alarm is generated with a signal change from 0 to 1 and an outgoing program alarm is generated with a signal change from 1 to 0. The program alarm is triggered synchronously to program execution.

You can append up to ten associated values to the program alarm at the parametersSD_i (0 ≤ i ≤10). The associated values are detected at the time of signal change at parameter SIG and assigned to the program alarm. For more information on configuring associated values, refer to the following sections: [Insert associated values in alarms](Configuring%20alarms.md#insert-associated-values-in-alarms-s7-1500), [Examples of associated values](Configuring%20alarms.md#examples-of-associated-values-s7-1500).

Each incoming or outgoing alarm is assigned a time stamp:

- The current system time of the PLC when the signal change occurs is used by default (default value at the TIMESTAMP parameter).
- If you wish to specify a different time stamp, you can create one at the parameter TIMESTAMP.

  The time value must always be specified in system time (i.e. UTC) as this is the time used for time synchronization throughout the plant.
- If an alarm is to be time-stamped with a local time, a conversion module, which converts the local time to system time, must be connected in series. This is the only way to make sure that the time stamp is shown properly in the alarm display.

To use the current system time of the CPU, set the TIMESTAMP parameter to its default value (LDT#1970-01-01-00:00:00.0).

#### Total size of the associated values for program messages

The alarm procedure of the S7-1500 CPU allows a maximum of 512 bytes for associated values (SD parameters) of the instruction "Program_Alarm".

For internal information, ProDiag requires part of this associated value total. This means that the three possible associated values in the specific text field can use a maximum of 400 bytes.

You will only see a warning regarding this number in the TIA Portal <= V14 during compilation. There is a stricter check as of TIA Portal V14 SP1. If this number is exceeded, an error is output during compilation to rule out error scenarios during runtime. This check from now on no longer refers to the actual size of the SD parameters at the time the instruction "Program_Alarm" is called, but to the maximum possible size of the SD parameters. This means you must decide beforehand with what length you declare the character string tags you are transferring, as these take up a large number of bytes.

#### Call of the instruction "Generate program alarm with associated values"

Call the instruction only in a function block (FB). The block is processed synchronously. An alarm is triggered as soon as the block is exited. If there is an error during processing, an error code is output.

Once the instruction has been inserted in the FB, a multi-instance of the data type "Program_Alarm" is created in the "Static" section of the block interface. Choose any name for this multi-instance in the dialog which appears. It is also the name of the program alarm.

Finally, add the parameters of the instruction in line with your requirements (see the "Parameters" table).

#### Configuring the program alarm

The program alarm settings are displayed in the "Properties" window when you select the name of the program alarm in the "Static" section or in the network of the FB. Select the alarm class, priority, etc. and edit the alarm text.

The settings that are made here can be edited in the project tree. To do this, open the "Alarms" tab under "PLC supervisions & alarms"; in it open the "Program alarms" tab. All existing program alarms are then displayed in the "Alarm types" table.

#### Parameters

The following table shows the parameters of the instruction "Generate program alarm with associated values":

| Parameter | Declaration | Data type | Memory area | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| SIG | Input | BOOL | I, Q, M, D, L, T, C or constant | The signal to be monitored.  - Positive signal edge: An incoming program alarm is generated - Negative signal edge: An outgoing program alarm is generated |
| TIMESTAMP | Input | LDT | M, D, L or constant | This parameter is used to assign an alarm a time stamp, for example, from an input signal with a distributed stamp. The time value must always be specified in system time (i.e. UTC), as this is the time used for time synchronization throughout the plant.  - "Not assigned" means that the system time of the CPU will be used as the interrupt time stamp when a signal change occurs (default). - Any system time input will be used as the interrupt time stamp when a signal change occurs.   Note: If an interrupt is to be time-stamped with a local time, a conversion module must be connected in series to convert the local time to system time. This is the only way to make sure that the time stamp is shown correctly in the interrupt display.  Note: When acknowledging the alarm, the PLC system time is always used as time stamp. |
| SD_i | Input | VARIANT | I, Q, M, D, L | i-th associated value (1 ≤ i ≤ 10)  You can use binary numbers, integers, floating-point numbers, or character strings as associated values. |
| Error | Output | BOOL | I, Q, M, D, L | Status parameter Error  Error = TRUE indicates that an error has occurred during processing. The possible error cause is displayed at the Status parameter. |
| Status | Output | WORD | I, Q, M, D, L | Status parameter Status  Display of error information (see "Error and Status parameters"). |

You can find additional information on valid data types under "See also".

#### Error and Status parameters

The following table contains all specific error information that is output via the Error and Status parameters.

| Error | Status* | Explanation |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 0 | 0000 | No error, or the instruction was not processed because there was no signal edge at the parameter SIG. |
| 1 | 0085 | "Only information" alarm type |
| 1 | 8001 | Invalid static alarm information |
| 1 | 8002 | No valid static alarm information |
| 1 | 8004 | Maximum size of 512 bytes reached for the associated values ​​of the alarm. |
| 1 | 8005 | A positive signal edge is pending at parameter SIG and there is still an alarm pending which has not yet been acknowledged. |
| 1 | 8007 | Outgoing alarm which was not preceded by an incoming alarm. |
| 1 | 8087 | Static alarms are deactivated. |
| 1 | 8089 | Alarm is too long. |
| 1 | 80Ax | The SD_i parameter has an invalid value. |
| 1 | 80C1 | The CPU does not generate any alarms at this time because initialization routines are running (this is the case after download in RUN, for example). Try again later. |
| 1 | 80C2 | The maximum number of alarms per time unit has been sent. Try again later. |
| 1 | 80C3 | All dynamic alarm instances are being used. Try again later. |
| 1 | 80C4 | Alarm is output and cannot be overwritten. Try again later. |
| * The error codes in the program editor are displayed as integer or hexadecimal values. For information on switching the display formats, refer to "See also". |  |  |

#### Example

In the following example, you generate a program alarm with associated value for a signal change.

Create one tag in a global data block for storing the signal value to be monitored.

![Example](images/70647840779_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

The instruction is called in a function block. Create four parameters for the function block for interconnection of the instruction.

![Example](images/70647849611_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

Interconnect the parameters of the instruction as follows.

![Example](images/70648110475_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

Call the function block in an OB.

![Example](images/70647973643_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

A PLC alarm is generated automatically when the instruction is created. Open the PLC supervisions & alarms dialog and select the alarm to be edited in the Alarms > Program alarms tab. Create the alarm text, including two key words in the Properties > Basic settings of the alarm.

Note: If you right-click in the text box, you can insert a key word, tag, or text list.

As a result of the character sequence "@1%s@", the value of parameter SD_1 ("#MyStaticText") is read out and output as a character string.

See also:

- [Insert associated values in alarms](Configuring%20alarms.md#insert-associated-values-in-alarms-s7-1500)
- [Examples of associated values](Configuring%20alarms.md#examples-of-associated-values-s7-1500)
- [Keywords for alarm texts](Configuring%20alarms.md#keywords-for-alarm-texts-s7-300-s7-400-s7-1500)

![Example](images/70704927755_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

If parameter SIG ("CreateAlarm") has the signal state "TRUE", the PLC alarm is output. At output parameter STATUS ("OUTPUT_Status"), the value "0001" indicates that a signal change has occurred. Then, it is indicated that no further processing is taking place (the value is "0000"). At parameter SD_1 ("#MyStaticText"), the associated value of the PLC alarm is output.

A time stamp can be transferred to parameter TIMESTAMP. If not interconnected, parameter TIMESTAMP outputs the local time of the CPU clock. At output parameter ERROR ("OUTPUT_Error"), it is indicated that the processing of the instruction was error-free.

![Example](images/70648119307_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

Use the web server of the CPU, for example, to output the PLC alarm. The following is necessary in order to use the web server:

- The web server must be activated in the CPU settings.

Use the Internet browser to open the Web server (via the IP address of the CPU) and log on in the Web server menu. The CPU outputs the alarm text as long as the signal being monitored ("CreateAlarm") supplies the value "TRUE".

![Example](images/70648192139_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Program code**

You can find additional information and the program code for the above named example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500)

#### Example

You can find a detailed application example in the [Siemens Industry Online Support](https://support.industry.siemens.com/cs/document/98210758?dti=0&lc=en-WW).
