## File handling (S7-1200, S7-1500)

This section contains information on the following topics:

- [FileReadC: Read file from Memory Card (S7-1200, S7-1500)](#filereadc-read-file-from-memory-card-s7-1200-s7-1500)
- [FileWriteC: Write file on the memory card (S7-1200, S7-1500)](#filewritec-write-file-on-the-memory-card-s7-1200-s7-1500)
- [FileDelete: Delete file on the memory card (S7-1500)](#filedelete-delete-file-on-the-memory-card-s7-1500)
- [Program example for file handling (S7-1500)](#program-example-for-file-handling-s7-1500)

### FileReadC: Read file from Memory Card (S7-1200, S7-1500)

#### Description

The "FileReadC" instruction is used to read data from a file located on the memory card and write it to a destination area on the CPU. You specify the file by entering its name and complete path.

Enable reading from the file using the REQ control parameter. The output parameters Done, Busy, Error and Status indicate the status of the job.

The following rules apply for the file name and path specification:

- The file name must not be longer than 55 characters.
- The following characters are permitted for the folder and file name: "0" to "9", "a" to "z", "A" to "Z", "-", "_"
- The file name must not contain more than one dot ("."); the dot separates the name from the file extension. The file name must contain at least one character. A file extension is not necessary.
- The path name must not start with "/", "\" or ".".
- The path name must not contain any ".."

Examples of valid paths and file names: "UserFiles/Lift16_DataBase.txt", "UserFiles/2017-04-13_ErrorLog.bin"

You can read up to 16 MB (16,777,216 bytes) by executing "FileReadC"; the data is read segment by segment. The segment size is device-specific; an S7-1500-CPU, for example, uses 32 KB (32,768 byte) blocks. If the number of data items to be read is greater than the segment size, you will need to call the instruction multiple times in your program. For consistency reasons, you should therefore not access the data read until after you call the instruction for the last time.

The parameters "Offset" and "Length" specify the length of the items of data to be read. The resource occupied is released again once the read process is complete.

#### Functional description

The "FileReadC" instruction works asynchronously. Processing takes place across several calls. You start processing with a rising edge at the parameter "REQ".

The parameters "Busy" and "Done" indicate the status of the job.

If an error occurs during execution, this is signaled by the parameters "Error" and "Status".

See also: [Difference between synchronous and asynchronous instructions](Asynchronous%20instructions%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#difference-between-synchronous-and-asynchronous-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500)

#### Parameters

The following table shows the parameters of the "FileReadC" instruction:

| Parameters | Declaration | Data type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| REQ | Input | BOOL | Control parameter Request  Enables reading a file from the memory card with a rising edge. |
| Name | Input | STRING | Name of the file to be read incl. complete path |
| Offset | Input | UDINT | Byte offset after which the file is to be read |
| Length | Input | UDINT | Length of the area to be read in bytes  Length = 0 means that the maximum possible number of data items per call is read (for a S7-1500-CPU, 32 KB or the size of the "Data" parameter) |
| Done | Output | BOOL | 1: The instruction has been executed successfully.  The information read has been transferred to the "Data" parameter. |
| Busy | Output | BOOL | Status parameter  - 0: Execution of the instruction completed or not yet started. - 1: Execution of the instruction not yet complete. |
| Error | Output | BOOL | Status parameter  - 0: No error. - 1: An error occurred during execution of the instruction. Detailed information is output via the "Status" parameter. |
| Status | Output | WORD | Error code |
| ResultLength | Output | UDINT | Length of data read in bytes |
| Data | InOut | VARIANT | Destination area for data read  Permitted data types: BYTE and Array of BYTE |

You can find additional information on valid data types under [Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types).

#### Parameter Status

| Error code* (W#16#...) | Explanation |
| --- | --- |
| 0 | No error |
| 7000 | No job processing active |
| 7001 | Start of job processing. Parameter Busy = 1, Done = 0. |
| 7002 | Intermediate call (REQ  irrelevant): Instruction already active; Busy has the value "1". |
| 0081 | Warning: Offset + Length is greater than the length of the file. Data is read from "Offset" to the end of the file. "Data" contains fewer data items than requested; "ResultLength" is shorter than "Length"; data past "ResultLength" in "Data" remains unchanged. |
| 8091 | Path does not exist or is invalid. |
| 8092 | The "Name" parameter is not of the data type "STRING", is too long or contains invalid characters. |
| 8093 | The "Offset" parameter points past the end of the file to be read. |
| 8094 | The "Length" parameter is larger than the maximum permitted value. For an S7-1500 CPU, for example, the maximum value permitted is 16 MB, i.e. 16,777,216 bytes. |
| 80A1 | Read error; the destination area specified by the "Data" parameter may be partly overwritten. |
| 80B1 | The destination area specified by the "Data" parameter is shorter than the length required at the "Length" parameter. |
| 80C0 | The file cannot be accessed (write-protected or blocked by another process). |
| 80C3 | The maximum number of simultaneously active FileReadC instructions has already been reached. |
| 8A24 | The "Data" points to an impermissible area, for example to the load memory or the local data. |
| 8A30 | The target range is write-protected, for example a write-protected DB. |
| 8A51 | Invalid data type of the "Data" parameter. |
| 8A52 | The tag at the Data parameter is insufficient. A part of the source area data may have been written. |
| * The error codes in the program editor are displayed as integer or hexadecimal values. For information on switching the display formats, refer to "See also". |  |

#### Example

You will find the example here: [Program example for FileReadC & FileWriteC](#program-example-for-file-handling-s7-1500).

You can find additional information and the program code for the example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

---

**See also**

[Switching display formats in the program status](Testing%20the%20user%20program.md#switching-display-formats-in-the-program-status)

### FileWriteC: Write file on the memory card (S7-1200, S7-1500)

#### Description

The "FileWriteC" instruction is used to write data located in a source area on the CPU to a file located in the "UserFiles" folder on the memory card. You specify this file by entering its name and complete path. If the file does not exist, it is created by the CPU in the "UserFiles" folder. The "Offset" parameter must be padded with zeros in this case, otherwise W#16#8093 is output at the "Status" parameter. If the "UserFiles" folder does not exist either, it is also created by the CPU; subfolders are not created – in this case, W#16#8091 is output at the "Status" parameter.

Enable writing to the file using the "REQ" control parameter. The output parameters "Done", "Busy", "Error" and "Status" indicate the status of the job.

The following rules apply for the file name and path specification:

- The file name must not be longer than 55 characters.
- The following characters are permitted for the folder and file name: "0" to "9", "a" to "z", "A" to "Z", "-", "_"
- The file name must not contain more than one dot ("."); the dot separates the name from the file extension. The file name must contain at least one character. A file extension is not necessary.
- The path name must not start with "/", "\" or ".".
- The path name must not contain any ".."

Examples of valid paths and file names: "UserFiles/Lift16_DataBase.txt", "UserFiles/2017-04-13_ErrorLog.bin"

You can write up to 16 MB (16,777,216 bytes) by executing "FileWriteC"; the data is written segment by segment. The segment size is device-specific; an S7-1500-CPU, for example, uses 32 KB (32,768 byte) blocks. If the number of data items to be written is greater than the segment size, you will need to call the instruction multiple times in your program. For consistency reasons, you should therefore not access the data written until after you call the instruction for the last time. If the available file is too short, it is extended to the required size.

The parameters "Offset" and "Length" specify the location in the file to which the data is to be written. The resource occupied is released again once the write process is complete.

The "FileWriteC" instruction only starts the write operation if the following condition is fulfilled: "Offset" + "Length" <= 16 MB

#### Functional description

The "FileWriteC" instruction works asynchronously. Processing takes place across several calls. You start processing with a rising edge at the parameter "REQ".

The parameters "Busy" and "Done" indicate the status of the job.

If an error occurs during execution, this is signaled by the parameters "Error" and "Status".

See also: [Difference between synchronous and asynchronous instructions](Asynchronous%20instructions%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#difference-between-synchronous-and-asynchronous-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500)

#### Parameters

The following table shows the parameters of the instruction "FileWriteC":

| Parameters | Declaration | Data type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| REQ | Input | BOOL | Control parameter Request  Enables writing a file on the memory card with a rising edge. |
| Name | Input | STRING | Name of the file to be written incl. complete path |
| Offset | Input | UDINT | Byte offset from which the file is to be written |
| Length | Input | UDINT | Length of the area to be written in bytes  "Length" = 0 means that the entire source area specified with the "Data" parameter is written. |
| Done | Output | BOOL | 1: The instruction has been executed successfully. |
| Busy | Output | BOOL | Status parameter  - 0: Execution of the instruction completed or not yet started. - 1: Execution of the instruction not yet complete. |
| Error | Output | BOOL | Status parameter  - 0: No error. - 1: An error occurred during execution of the instruction. Detailed information is output via the "Status" parameter. |
| Status | Output | WORD | Error code |
| ResultLength | Output | UDINT | Length of data written in bytes |
| Data | InOut | VARIANT | Source area  Permitted data types: BYTE and Array of BYTE |

You can find additional information on valid data types under [Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types).

#### Parameter Status

| Error code* (W#16#...) | Explanation |
| --- | --- |
| 0 | No error |
| 7000 | No job processing active |
| 7001 | Start of job processing. Parameter Busy = 1, Done = 0. |
| 7002 | Intermediate call (REQ  irrelevant): Instruction already active; Busy has the value "1". |
| 8091 | Path does not exist or is invalid. |
| 8092 | The "Name" parameter is not of the data type "STRING", is too long or contains invalid characters. |
| 8093 | - The "Offset" parameter points past the end of the file to be written. - The creation of the file is rejected because "Offset" is greater than zero. |
| 8094 | - "Length" is greater than the maximum permitted value. For an S7-1500 CPU, for example, the maximum value permitted is 16 MB, i.e. 16,777,216 bytes. - "Length" + "Offset" is greater than the maximum permitted value. |
| 80A1 | Write error; the data in the file on the memory card may be partly overwritten. |
| 80B1 | The source area specified by the "Data" parameter is shorter than the length required at the "Length" parameter. |
| 80B3 | There is not enough space on the memory card or in the internal load memory. |
| 80B4 | The memory card or file is write-protected. |
| 80C0 | The file cannot be accessed. |
| 80C3 | The maximum number of simultaneously active FileWriteC instructions has already been reached. |
| 8A24 | The "Data" points to an impermissible area, for example to the load memory or the local data. |
| 8A51 | Invalid data type of the "Data" parameter. |
| 8A52 | The tag at the Data parameter is insufficient. A part of the source area data may have been written. |
| * The error codes in the program editor are displayed as integer or hexadecimal values. For information on switching the display formats, refer to "See also". |  |

#### Example

You will find the example here: [Program example for FileReadC & FileWriteC](#program-example-for-file-handling-s7-1500).

You can find additional information and the program code for the example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

---

**See also**

[Switching display formats in the program status](Testing%20the%20user%20program.md#switching-display-formats-in-the-program-status)

### FileDelete: Delete file on the memory card (S7-1500)

#### Validity

The "FileDelete" instruction is contained in S7-1500 CPUs as of firmware version V2.8.

#### Description

You use the "FileDelete" instruction to delete an existing file from the memory card.

The file must not be open. Wildcards in the "Name" parameter are not supported, meaning that names such as "UserFiles/*.txt" and "UserFiles/?.txt" are not permissible.

The "FileDelete" instruction is only permitted in the "Recipes" and "UserFiles" folders. Folders within these folders are possible, e.g. "UserFiles/Test/file1.txt".

> **Note**
>
> **Deleting data logs**
>
> Deleting a file in the "DataLog" folder with the "FileDelete" instruction is not permissible. You have to use the "DataLogDelete" instruction to delete data logs.

#### Functional description

"FileDelete" is an asynchronous instruction. Processing can extend over multiple calls. You start processing with a rising edge at the parameter "REQ".

The parameters "Busy" and "Done" indicate the status of the job.

If an error occurs during execution, this is signaled by the parameters "Error" and "Status".

See also: [Difference between synchronous and asynchronous instructions](Asynchronous%20instructions%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#difference-between-synchronous-and-asynchronous-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500)

#### Parameters

The following table shows the parameters of the "FileDelete" instruction.

| Parameters | Declaration | Data type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| REQ | Input | BOOL | Control parameter Request  Processing is started on a rising edge at REQ. |
| Name | Input | STRING | Path and name of the file to be deleted |
| Done | Output | BOOL | Status parameter  - 1: The instruction has been executed successfully. |
| Busy | Output | BOOL | Status parameter  - 0: The instruction is currently not being executed. - 1: The instruction is currently being executed. |
| Error | Output | BOOL | Status parameter  - 0: No error - 1: An error occurred during execution of the instruction. For detailed information, see the "Status" parameter. |
| Status | Output | WORD | Error code |

You can find additional information on valid data types under [Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types)

#### Parameter Status

| Error code* (W#16#...) | Explanation |
| --- | --- |
| 0000 | Instruction finished successfully |
| 7000 | No job processing active |
| 7001 | Start of job processing: Busy = 1, Done = 0. |
| 7002 | Intermediate call (REQ  irrelevant): Instruction already active; Busy has the value "1" |
| 8090 | File locked, e.g. file is open |
| 8091 | Path does not exist or is invalid. |
| 8092 | File does not exist in the path |
| 80A2 | Write error |
| 80A3 | The file is too large (>= 2147483648 bytes) and cannot be deleted using "FileDelete". |
| 80B4 | The memory card is write-protected |
| 80C3 | The maximum number of simultaneously active FileDelete instructions has already been reached |
| * The error codes in the program editor are displayed as integer or hexadecimal values. For information on switching the display formats, refer to "See also". |  |

---

**See also**

[Switching display formats in the program status](Testing%20the%20user%20program.md#switching-display-formats-in-the-program-status)

### Program example for file handling (S7-1500)

In the following example, you create a file on the memory card of a S7-1500 CPU and write data to this file. You then read out the data in this file. Delete the file afterward.

#### Storage of data

To store the data, create a tag and two structures for the "FileWriteC", "FileReadC" and "FileDelete" instructions in a global data block.

![Storage of data](images/135162625675_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

The data for writing the file is as follows.

![Storage of data](images/120220516619_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

#### Interconnecting parameters

Call the "FileWriteC", "FileReadC" and "FileDelete" instructions in an FB. Create the following local tags in the FB:

![Interconnecting parameters](images/135162744587_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 1**: Interconnect the parameters of the "FileWriteC" instruction as follows.

![Interconnecting parameters](images/135162748299_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 2:** If an error occurs, save the status of "FileWriteC" as follows.

![Interconnecting parameters](images/135162814603_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 3**: Interconnect the parameters of the "FileReadC" instruction as follows.

![Interconnecting parameters](images/135162752011_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 4:** If an error occurs, save the status of "FileReadC" as follows.

![Interconnecting parameters](images/135162822923_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 5**: Interconnect the parameters of the "FileDelete" instruction as follows.

![Interconnecting parameters](images/135162831243_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

**Network 6:** If an error occurs, save the status of "FileDelete" as follows.

![Interconnecting parameters](images/135162877963_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

#### Result of network 1 (FileWriteC)

If the normally open contact ("fileWrite.start") supplies the signal state "TRUE" and the normally closed contacts ("fileRead.busy") and ("fileDelete.busy") supply the signal state "FALSE", the "FileWriteC" instruction is executed.

The file to be written including the complete path is named at the input parameter NAME ("name"). Since a file with this name and path does not yet exist on the memory card, the file is created by the CPU. The file is then written from the first byte according to the value "0" of the input parameter OFFSET ("fileWrite.offset"). With the value "0" of the input parameter LENGTH ("fileWrite.length"), all data of the source area - stored via the DATA parameter ("fileWrite.data") - is written to the file on the memory card.

The success status of the processing of the "FileWriteC" instruction is output via the output parameter DONE ("#writeDone"). The length of the written data (16 bytes) is output at the output parameter RESULTLENGTH ("#writeResultLength"). When FileWriteC has been successfully executed, the value of the "#writeDone" tag (in the "fileWrite.done" tag) and the value of the "#writeResultLength" (in the "fileWrite.resultLength") tag are also stored. In addition, the "fileWrite.start" tag is reset to the value "FALSE".

![Result of network 1 (FileWriteC)](images/135162806923_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

#### Result of network 2 (FileWriteC)

In case of an error with the "FileWriteC" instruction ("#writeError" is TRUE), the status ("fileWrite.status") is stored in the "fileWrite.memErrStatus" tag.

The output parameter STATUS ("fileWrite.status") indicates that the processing in the example ran without errors.

#### Result of network 3 (FileReadC)

If the normally open contact ("fileRead.start") supplies the signal state "TRUE" and the normally closed contacts ("fileDelete.done"), ("fileDelete.busy") and ("fileWrite.busy") supply the signal state "FALSE", the "FileReadC" instruction is executed.

The file to be read including the complete path is named at the input parameter NAME ("name"). The file on the memory card is read from the first byte according to the value "0" of the input parameter OFFSET ("fileRead.offset"). With the value "0" of the input parameter LENGTH ("fileRead.length"), the maximum possible number of data per call are read.

The read data are then stored via the DATA parameter ("fileRead.data").

![Result of network 3 (FileReadC)](images/120220644747_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

The success status of the processing of the "FileReadC" instruction is output via the output parameter DONE ("#readDone"). The length of the read data (16 bytes) is output at the output parameter RESULTLENGTH ("#readResultLength"). When FileReadC has been successfully executed, the value of the "#readDone" tag (in the "fileRead.done" tag) and the value of the "#readResultLength" (in the "fileRead.resultLength") tag are also stored. In addition, the "fileRead.start" tag is reset to the value "FALSE".

![Result of network 3 (FileReadC)](images/135162810635_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

#### Result of network 4 (FileReadC)

In case of an error with the "FileReadC" instruction ("#readError" is TRUE), the status ("fileRead.status") is stored in the "fileRead.memErrStatus" tag.

The output parameter STATUS ("fileRead.status") indicates that the processing in the example ran without errors.

#### Result of network 5 (FileDelete)

If the normally open contact ("fileDelete.start") supplies the signal state "TRUE" and the normally closed contacts ("fileWrite.busy") and ("fileRead.busy") supply the signal state "FALSE", the "FileDelete" instruction is executed.

The respective file including the complete path is named at the input parameter NAME ("name"). The "FileDelete" instruction calls the file and deletes it from the memory card.

The success status of the processing of the "FileDelete" instruction is output via the output parameter DONE ("#deleteDone") and stored in the "fileDelete.done" tag. In addition, the "fileDelete.start" tag is reset to the value "FALSE".

![Result of network 5 (FileDelete)](images/135162974347_DV_resource.Stream@PNG-de-DE.png)

#### Result of network 6 (FileDelete)

In case of an error with the "FileDelete" instruction ("#deleteError" is TRUE), the status ("fileDelete.status") is stored in the "fileDelete.memErrStatus" tag.

The output parameter STATUS ("fileDelete.status") indicates that the processing in the example ran without errors.

#### Program code

You can find additional information and the program code for the example here: [Sample Library for Instructions](Example%20libraries%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#sample-library-for-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500).

---

**See also**

[FileReadC: Read file from Memory Card (S7-1200, S7-1500)](#filereadc-read-file-from-memory-card-s7-1200-s7-1500)
  
[FileWriteC: Write file on the memory card (S7-1200, S7-1500)](#filewritec-write-file-on-the-memory-card-s7-1200-s7-1500)

## Cryptography (S7-1500)

This section contains information on the following topics:

- [Random: Generate random number (S7-1500)](#random-generate-random-number-s7-1500)
- [SHA2: Form hash value using SHA2 (S7-1500)](#sha2-form-hash-value-using-sha2-s7-1500)

### Random: Generate random number (S7-1500)

#### Description

The "Random" instruction is used to generate a 32-bit random number.

The "Random" instruction does not provide any error information.

#### Parameters

The following table shows the parameters of the "Random" instruction:

| Parameters | Declaration | Data type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Ret_Val | Return | UDINT | 32-bit random number |

You can find additional information on valid data types under "[Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types)".

### SHA2: Form hash value using SHA2 (S7-1500)

#### Hash function and hash value

A hash function is a mathematical function. It converts data of any length into strings of fixed length. The result of this conversion is called a hash value. The characters that are allowed for the hash value are determined via the hash function.

A hash function has the following properties:

- Uniqueness: Identical data has the same hash value.
- Irreversibility: It must not be possible to recover the original data from the calculated hash value.
- Collision-free: Different data also has different hash values.

One can therefore verify the identity of two data areas by comparing their hash values. This makes it possible, for example, to determine whether data has been falsified during a data transmission.

#### Definition: SHA

The abbreviation SHA stands for Secure Hash Algorithm. The secure hash algorithm refers to a group of standardized hash functions.

SHA1 was a very important cryptographic hash function in the 1990s. It has been considered insecure since 2004.

SHA2 differs from SHA1 in several features and functions. SHA2 includes SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-384 and SHA-512. The number after the hyphen indicates the length of the hash value in bits.

#### Description

The "SHA2" instruction calculates a SHA2 hash value from a given data area. The length of the resulting hash value is defined by the "HashLen" parameter. The following values are supported: 224, 256, 384 and 512.

The calculation is made according to FIPS PUB 180-4. The SHA-224 algorithm is used for the hash value 224, and the SHA-256 algorithm for the hash value 256.

The size of the data area is not limited, because only data segments with a maximum length of 8192 bytes are used internally by the CPU for calculation. If the data area is larger than 8192 bytes, it is divided by the CPU into smaller segments. The hash value is delivered only after the processing of "SHA2" is completed.

> **Note**
>
> **Validity of the hash value**
>
> The "Data" data area must not be changed until the "SHA2" instruction has been completed.
>
> The generated hash value is correct only if this rule is observed.

#### System resources

The maximum number of "simultaneously" active SHA2 instructions can be found in the following table:

|  | Maximum number of "simultaneously" active SHA2 instructions |
| --- | --- |
| S7-1500-CPU | 1 |

#### Functional description

The "SHA2" instruction is an asynchronous instruction. Processing extends over multiple calls. You start processing with a rising edge at the "REQ" parameter.

The parameters "Busy" and "Done" indicate the status of the job.

If an error occurs during execution, this is signaled by the parameters "Error" and "Status".

See also: [Difference between synchronous and asynchronous instructions](Asynchronous%20instructions%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#difference-between-synchronous-and-asynchronous-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500)

#### Parameters

The following table shows the parameters of the "SHA2" instruction:

| Parameters | Declaration | Data type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| REQ | Input | BOOL | Control parameter Request  Activates the job on a rising edge |
| Data | Input | VARIANT | Data from which the hash value is to be calculated  All data types except "BOOL" and "Array of BOOL" are permitted.  When transferring the data from or to another computer system, you need to ensure that the data type there is the same as on the PLC. |
| DataLen | Input | UDINT | Length of the data area from which the hash value is to be calculated, in bytes If you specify 0 here, the length of the tag pointed to by the "Data" parameter is used. |
| HashLen | Input | UINT | Length of the hash value to be calculated in bits  The following values are supported: 224, 256, 384 and 512 |
| Done | Output | BOOL | Status parameter:  - 0: Job not yet started or still being processed. - 1: Job completed without error. This state is only displayed for one call. |
| Busy | Output | BOOL | Status parameter:  - 0: Job not yet started or already completed. - 1: Job not yet completed. A new job with this instance cannot be started. |
| Error | Output | BOOL | Status parameter:  - 0: No error occurred. - 1: Error occurred during processing. The "Status" parameter supplies detailed information on the type of error. This state is only displayed for one call. |
| Status | Output | WORD | Error information |
| Hash | InOut | VARIANT | Hash value  Permitted data type: Array of BYTE |

You can find more information on valid data types under "[Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types)".

#### "Status" parameter

| STATUS (W#16#...) | Explanation |
| --- | --- |
| 0000 | "SHA2" was completed without errors. |
| 7000 | No job processing active |
| 7001 | Start of job execution |
| 7002 | Intermediate call (REQ irrelevant): |
| 8080 | "DataLen" does not match the length of the area pointed to by the "Data" parameter. |
| 8082 | "HashLen" does not match the length of the area to which the "Hash" parameter points. |
| 8083 | The length in VARIANT of the "Data" parameter was changed during processing, e.g. by a download.  Note: You must ensure that the content of the "Data" parameter is not changed while "SHA2" is being processed. "SHA2" namely does not monitor whether the content of "Data" is changed; such monitoring would significantly reduce the performance of the PLC. If the content of "Data" is changed, "SHA2" is still executed, but the calculated hash value is not meaningful. |
| 80A3 | The value of the "HashLen" parameter is not supported. |
| 80C3 | Insufficient resources or temporary memory bottleneck |
| 80E5 | Internal error |
| 8224 | The "Data" parameter points to an impermissible area, for example to the load memory or the local data. |
| 8924 | The "Hash" parameter points to an impermissible area, for example to the load memory or the local data. |
| 8251 | The "BOOL" and "Array of BOOL" data types are not permitted for the "Data" parameter. |
| 8951 | Incorrect data type for the "Hash" parameter. Only the "Array of BYTE" data type is permitted. |
| 8252 | The variable at the "DATA" parameter is NULL or cannot be accessed because, for example, the DB is too small or does not exist. |
| 8952 | The variable at the "Hash" parameter is NULL or cannot be accessed because, for example, the DB is too small or does not exist. |
| xxyy, xx > 80 | For general error information, refer to [Evaluating errors with output parameter RET_VAL](Programming%20basics.md#evaluating-errors-with-output-parameter-ret_val) |

## R/H system (S7-1500)

This section contains information on the following topics:

- [RH_CTRL: Influencing sequences in R/H systems (S7-1500)](#rh_ctrl-influencing-sequences-in-rh-systems-s7-1500)
- [RH_GetPrimaryID: Determining the redundancy ID of the primary CPU (S7-1500)](#rh_getprimaryid-determining-the-redundancy-id-of-the-primary-cpu-s7-1500)

### RH_CTRL: Influencing sequences in R/H systems (S7-1500)

#### Description

With the instruction "RH_CTRL", you can influence R/H systems as follows:

- You can disable the system state "SYNCUP". The lock is in force until you revoke it with the "RH_CTRL" instruction or until the R/H system goes into "STOP" mode.
- You can re-enable the system state "SYNCUP".
- You can request the system state "SYNCUP".
- You can request that the primary CPU goes into "STOP" mode.
- You can request that the backup CPU goes into "STOP" mode.
- You can query whether the "SYNCUP" system state is currently locked.
- You can request that one of the two R/H-CPUs goes into the "STOP" mode and takes on the role of the backup CPU.

  This is required if the R/H system is in a non-defined state, for example when both redundancy connections were interrupted at the same time and both R/H-CPUs have taken on the role of the primary CPU.
- You can specify that the "SYNCUP" system state be run through without the intended MRP check when there is at least one MRP ring present. (MRP check in this context means the following: A check is carried out to determine whether the MRP ring in which the backup CPU is located is closed. If there is exactly one MRP ring, it is checked. If there are two MRP rings present, the one with the backup CPU is checked.)

  | Symbol | Meaning |
  | --- | --- |
  |  | **Danger** |
  | **Prevent bodily injury and property damage**  Note that running through the "SYNCUP" system state without an MRP check can: - Endanger human life or health - Cause errors in IO devices, dangerous operating states and damage to the entire system. It is therefore strongly recommended that the transition to the "SYNCUP" system state without a prior MRP check be carried out only in commissioning scenarios and not during productive operation of your system. |  |

#### Functional description

The "RH_CTRL" instruction works asynchronously. Processing extends over multiple calls. You start the job by calling "RH_CTRL" with REQ = 1.

The output parameters RET_VAL and BUSY indicate the status of the job.

See also: [Difference between synchronous and asynchronous instructions](Asynchronous%20instructions%20%28S7-1200%2C%20S7-1500%29.md#difference-between-synchronous-and-asynchronous-instructions-s7-1200-s7-1500)

> **Note**
>
> **Synchronous execution**
>
> If the job can be processed immediately, the value 0 is displayed at the output parameter BUSY after the first call.

#### Parameter

The following table shows the parameters of the instruction "RH_CTRL":

| Parameter | Declaration | Data type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| REQ | Input | BOOL | Control parameter Request  Enables the execution of the instruction when REQ = "1". |
| Mode | Input | BYTE | Using the "Mode" parameter, select how you want to influence the R/H system:  - 3: Disable execution of the SYNCUP (as of firmware version V2.6 of the S7-1500-R/H CPUs) - 4:   - Re-enable execution of the SYNCUP (as of firmware version V2.6 of the S7-1500-R/H CPUs)   - If applicable, switch on the intended MRP check in "SYNCUP" system state again (as of firmware version V3.1 of the S7-1500-R/H CPUs) - 7: Request SYNCUP system state (as of firmware version V2.9 of S7-1500-R/H CPUs) - 8: Request STOP of the primary CPU (as of firmware version V2.9 of the S7-1500-R/H CPUs) - 9: Request STOP of the backup CPU (as of firmware version V2.9 of S7-1500-R/H CPUs) - 10: Query whether the system state SYNCUP is currently locked (from firmware version V3.0 of the S7-1500-R/H CPUs) - 11: Request STOP of an R/H-CPU and assign the role of the backup CPU to it (as of firmware version V3.1 of the S7-1500 R/H-CPUs)   Requirement: The corresponding R/H-CPU is in "RUN" mode and there is no pairing. - 14: Switch off the intended MRP check in "SYNCUP" system state (as of firmware version V3.1 of the S7-1500-R/H CPUs) |
| Submode | Input | WORD | Hidden parameter. It must have the value 0. |
| Ret_Val | Output | INT | Error code |
| Busy | Output | BOOL | Status parameter  - 0: Execution of the instruction complete. - 1: Execution of the instruction not yet complete. |

You can find additional information on valid data types under "[Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types)".

#### Ret_Val parameter

| Error code* (W#16#...) | Explanation |
| --- | --- |
| 0 | No error. |
| 1 | - with mode = 3: The "SYNCUP" system state is already disabled. - with mode = 4: The "SYNCUP" system state is already enabled. - with Mode=14: The intended MRP check in "SYNCUP" system state is already switched off. |
| 3 | The "SYNCUP" system state was disabled by "RH_CTRL" at an earlier time and is still disabled. |
| 4 | Currently there is no disable of the "SYNCUP" system state caused by "RH_CTRL". The intended MRP check in "SYNCUP" system state is switched on. |
| 14 | Currently there is no disable of the "SYNCUP" system state caused by "RH_CTRL". An MRP check does not take place in "SYNCUP" system state. |
| 7000 | No job in progress (REQ = 0). |
| 7001 | REQ = 1 at first call: Data transfer triggered; BUSY has the value "1". |
| 7002 | Intermediate call (REQ  irrelevant): Data transfer already active; BUSY has the value 1. |
| 8082 | Disabling the "SYNCUP" system state is no longer possible, because the R/H system is already in this system state. |
| 8090 | Invalid value of the "Mode" parameter. |
| 8091 | Invalid value of the "Submode" parameter. It must have the value 0. |
| 80A0 | The "SYNCUP" system state cannot be requested:  - The R/H system is not in the "RUN-Solo" system state. - The R/H system is in the "RUN-Solo" system state, but there is no successful pairing between the CPUs. |
| 80A1 | The R/H system is not in the RUN-Redundant system state. |
| 80A2 | The following requirement is not fulfilled: The R/H-CPU is in "RUN" mode and there is no pairing. |
| * The error codes in the program editor are displayed as integer or hexadecimal values. For information on switching the display formats, refer to "See also". |  |

#### Request system state "SYNCUP"

Requirements for requesting the "SYNCUP" system state:

- The S7-1500R/H system is in the "RUN-Solo" system state.
- A successful pairing exists between the CPUs.

This means that the primary CPU is in "RUN" mode and the backup CPU is in "STOP" mode.

If you call the "RH_CTRL" instruction with Mode=7, the "SYNCUP" system state is requested.

If all the requirements for reaching the redundant system state (e.g. hardware switch is set to RUN, memory card inserted) are met, the R/H system goes into the "SYNCUP" system state, runs through it and reaches the "RUN-Redundant" system state.

You receive the following feedback from the operating system:

- If one of the requirements for the request of the "SYNCUP" system state is not met, "RH_CTRL" returns the error code 0x80A0.
- If the "SYNCUP" system state could be requested successfully, "RH_CTRL" returns the error code 0. An entry is made in the diagnostic buffer on both CPUs.
- If the "SYNCUP" system state cannot be started or run through successfully (for example, because the hardware switch on the backup CPU is set to STOP or no memory card is inserted or the execution of "SYNCUP" is disabled or an MRP ring is open), "RH_CTRL" also returns the error code 0. A corresponding entry is made in the diagnostic buffer on both CPUs.

#### Request STOP of the primary CPU

Requirement for the request that the primary CPU go into the "STOP" mode:

- The S7-1500R/H system is in the "RUN-Redundant" system state.

If you call the "RH_CTRL" instruction with Mode=8, the following happens:

- The previous backup CPU becomes the primary CPU and is then in "RUN" mode. On it, "RH_CTRL" returns the error code 0.
- The previous primary CPU becomes the backup CPU and is then in "STOP" mode.

The R/H system is now in the "RUN-Solo" system state with the CPU roles swapped.

After a successful switchover, both CPUs enter the redundancy error and a request initiated by the system in their diagnostic buffer. This indicates the change in the mode ("RUN-Redundant" to "STOP" or "RUN-Redundant" to "RUN").

If the R/H system is in the "RUN-Solo" or "SYNCUP" system state, a call of "RH_CTRL" with Mode=8 has no effect on the system state; only the error code 0x80A1 is returned.

If you intend to request the "SYNCUP" system state after the STOP of the primary CPU, you must ensure that a new change to the "SYNCUP" system state is possible (this means it is not prevented, for example, because of an open MRP ring) before calling "RH_CTRL" with Mode=8. One possible approach is as follows:

1. Check the diagnostic status of your system by calling the "GET_DIAG" instruction. Check that there are no errors or maintenance demanded or maintenance required.
2. Check whether the "SYNCUP" system state has been disabled by calling "RH_CTRL" with Mode=3.

> **Note**
>
> **Difference to Failover**
>
> The transition of the R/H system from the "RUN-Redundant" system state to the "RUN-Solo" system state with swapped CPU roles is not identical to a so-called Failover. With Failover the primary CPU is also changed, but the previous primary CPU is shut down or it fails.

> **Note**
>
> **Change of the primary CPU with a redundancy connection**
>
> The transition of the R/H system from the "RUN-Redundant" system state to the "RUN-Solo" system state with swapped CPU roles is also possible when there is only one redundancy connection.

> **Note**
>
> **Calling "RH_CTRL" with Mode=8 while the download is in progress**
>
> If your R/H system is in the "RUN-Redundant" system state and a download is currently active, the call of "RH_CTRL" with Mode=8 has the same effect as if you set the primary CPU to STOP.

#### Request STOP of backup CPU

Requirement for the request that the backup CPU go into the "STOP" mode:

- The S7-1500R/H system is in the "RUN-Redundant" system state.

If you call the "RH_CTRL" instruction with Mode=9, the following happens:

- The previous primary CPU remains the primary CPU and goes into "RUN" mode. On it, "RH_CTRL" returns the error code 0.
- The previous backup CPU remains the backup CPU and goes into "STOP" mode.

Therefore, the R/H system is now in the "RUN-Solo" system state with unchanged CPU roles.

After a successful switchover, both CPUs enter the redundancy error and a request initiated by the system in their diagnostic buffer. This indicates the change in the mode ("RUN-Redundant" to "RUN" or "RUN-Redundant" to "STOP").

If the R/H system is in the "RUN-Solo" or "SYNCUP" system state, a call of "RH_CTRL" with Mode=9 has no effect on the system state; only the error code 0x80A1 is returned.

If you intend to request the "SYNCUP" system state after changing the primary CPU, you must ensure that the "SYNCUP" system state is not locked (e.g. because of an open MRP ring) before calling "RH_CTRL" with Mode=9. One possible approach is as follows:

1. Check the diagnostic status of your system by calling the "GET_DIAG" instruction. Check that there are no errors or maintenance demanded or maintenance required.
2. Check whether the "SYNCUP" system state has been disabled by calling "RH_CTRL" with Mode=3.

> **Note**
>
> **Calling "RH_CTRL" with Mode=9 while the download is in progress**
>
> If your R/H system is in the "RUN-Redundant" system state and a download is currently active, the call of "RH_CTRL" with Mode=9 has the same effect as if you set the backup CPU to STOP.

#### Sample program for modes 7, 8 and 9

The code below is called cyclically, for example in OB1. It repeatedly requests the change of the primary CPU and the subsequent SYNCUP. The requirement for the backup CPU to become the primary CPU is that there are no errors or maintenance demanded or maintenance required.

//temporary variables

VAR_TEMP

   RetVal : Int;

   CNT_DIAG_temp : UInt;

   DIS : DIS;

   DNN : DNN;

   busy : Bool;

   subordinateState: UInt;

END_VAR

//Get diag state of R/H system.

//If #subordinateState is 0,

//there is no diagnostics active,

//so we also know the MRP-ring is closed.

#RetVal := GET_DIAG(MODE := 2, LADDR := "Local1~RHSystem", CNT_DIAG => #CNT_DIAG_temp, DIAG := #DNN);

#subordinateState := #DNN.SubordinateState;

//Get system operating state

//If #DIS.OperatingState = 40, R/H system is in RUN-Redundant

//If #DIS.OperatingState = 37, R/H system is in RUN-Solo

#RetVal := GET_DIAG(MODE := 1, LADDR := "Local1~RHSystem", CNT_DIAG => #CNT_DIAG_temp, DIAG := #DIS);

//If R/H system is in RUN-Redundant and no diagnostics is active

// => Trigger STOP of primary CPU

//If R/H system is in RUN-Solo => Trigger syncup

//In any other state: Do nothing

IF #DIS.OperatingState = 40 AND subordinateState = 0 THEN

   #RetVal := RH_CTRL(REQ := 1, Mode := 8, Submode := 0, Busy => #busy);

ELSEIF #DIS.OperatingState = 37 THEN

   #RetVal := RH_CTRL(REQ := 1, Mode := 7, Submode := 0, Busy => #busy);

END_IF

#### Request STOP of an R/H-CPU and assign the role of the backup CPU to it

This is required if the R/H system is in a non-defined state, for example when both redundancy connections were interrupted at the same time and both R/H-CPUs have taken on the role of the primary CPU.

After a successful execution of "RH_CTRL" the R/H system is in a defined state again: There is a primary CPU which is in the "RUN" operating state and a backup CPU which is in the "STOP" operating state. There is no pairing.

> **Note**
>
> **Execution of "RH_CTRL" on exactly one R/H-CPU**
>
> Ensure that "RH_CTRL" is only performed on one R/H-CPU. If "RH_CTRL" is performed on both R/H-CPUs, the system state goes to "STOP".  
>  To ensure that "RH_CTRL" is only performed on one CPU, you can, for example, determine the redundancy ID of the primary CPU b using the instruction "RH_GetPrimaryID" and then stop the R/H-CPU with the redundancy ID 1.

#### Running through the system state "SYNCUP" without an MRP check

If you call the "RH_CTRL" instruction with Mode=14 and the instruction is run through without an error, a corresponding entry is made in the diagnostic buffer (incoming event) and the maintenance LED is lit to indicate that the MRP check is now switched off (it is also lit during redundant operation.). The associated entry for the outgoing event is made when the MRP check is switched on again.

The operating system switches on the intended MRP check in system state "SYNCUP" again in the following cases:

- The CPU goes into STOP state.
- You call "RH_CTRL" with Mode = 3.
- You call "RH_CTRL" with Mode = 4.

The following applies in regard to replacement part compatibility: Mode 14 does not run in projects that were created on S7-1500-R/H CPUs with firmware version < V3.1. This also applies when you run such a project on an S7-1500-R/H CPU with firmware version >= V3.1.

---

**See also**

[Switching display formats in the program status](Testing%20the%20user%20program.md#switching-display-formats-in-the-program-status)

### RH_GetPrimaryID: Determining the redundancy ID of the primary CPU (S7-1500)

#### Description

The "RH_GetPrimaryID" instruction is used to determine the redundancy ID of the primary CPU.

You can use this instruction in all system states even if the pairing of the two R or H CPUs has not yet been performed.

#### Parameter

The following table shows the parameters of the instruction "RH_GetPrimaryID":

| Parameter | Declaration | Data type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Ret_Val | Output | INT | Return value |

You can find additional information on valid data types under: [Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types)

#### Ret_Val parameter

| Error code* (W#16#...) | Explanation |
| --- | --- |
| 1 | The CPU with redundancy ID 1 is the primary CPU. |
| 2 | The CPU with redundancy ID 2 is the primary CPU. |
| 8091 | No data available, for example because "RH_GetPrimaryID" is not supported by your CPU. |
| * The error codes in the program editor are displayed as integer or hexadecimal values. For information on switching the display formats, refer to "See also". |  |

---

**See also**

[Switching display formats in the program status](Testing%20the%20user%20program.md#switching-display-formats-in-the-program-status)

## Safety extensions (S7-1200, S7-1500)

This section contains information on the following topics:

- [ACK_FCT_WARN: Acknowledge warning message for exceeding the F-cycle time (S7-1200, S7-1500)](#ack_fct_warn-acknowledge-warning-message-for-exceeding-the-f-cycle-time-s7-1200-s7-1500)

### ACK_FCT_WARN: Acknowledge warning message for exceeding the F-cycle time (S7-1200, S7-1500)

#### Description

You can use the "ACK_FCT_WARN" instruction to acknowledge the message in the diagnostic buffer that warns you about exceeding the F-cycle time. The F-cycle time is the maximum time that may pass between two F-OB calls.

If the instruction at input parameter "ACK_WARN" detects a rising edge, the warning message is acknowledged. In all other cases it is not acknowledged.

You can find more information on the F-cycle time in the "SIMATIC Safety - Configuring and Programming" Operating Manual.

#### Parameters

The following table shows the parameters of the "ACK_FCT_WARN" instruction:

| Parameter | Declaration | Data type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| ACK_WARN | Input | BOOL | Acknowledgment of the warning message on rising edge |

You can find additional information on valid data types under: [Overview of the valid data types](Data%20types.md#overview-of-the-valid-data-types)
